‚óŹ The playhead is rendered by a waveform/timeline component using
  :current-time="videoPlayerRef?.currentTime || 0" (SotModal.vue:576). The
  problem is that videoPlayerRef?.currentTime is read once and isn't reactive ‚ÄĒ
  Vue doesn't re-render when the raw <video> element's currentTime changes
  during playback. Let me look at the surrounding context.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Confirmed the bug. :current-time="videoPlayerRef?.currentTime || 0" reads
  currentTime directly off the raw DOM video element. That property is not 
  reactive ‚ÄĒ Vue has no way to know it changed during playback, so the playhead
  only updates when something else triggers a re-render (e.g. the @timeupdate ‚Üí
  updateTimecode updating currentTimecode, which... let me verify whether that
  even causes this template region to re-render).

  The clean fix: bind :current-time to a reactive ref that's updated by the
  @timeupdate handler (and ideally a requestAnimationFrame loop for smoothness,
  since timeupdate only fires ~4x/sec). Let me check the composable's
  updateTimecode and what's exposed.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Now I understand the architecture fully. The composable already updates
  currentTimecode on @timeupdate. The cleanest fix that fits the existing
  pattern: add a reactive currentTimeSec ref to the composable, update it in
  updateTimecode(), and bind the waveform's :current-time to it.

  But timeupdate only fires ~4x/second, which would make the playhead choppy.
  Let me check the top of the composable to see the refs declared, then decide.
  Let me read lines 60‚Äď90 and the _attachTimeUpdate / element-binding area.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ I have a complete picture. The fix:

  1. Add a reactive currentTimeSec ref to the composable.
  2. Drive it with a requestAnimationFrame loop while playing (smooth playhead,
  ~60fps) and also update on timeupdate/seek (covers paused scrubbing/stepping).
  3. Bind the waveform :current-time to currentTimeSec instead of the
  non-reactive DOM read.

  Let me make the composable changes. First the ref declaration and a RAF loop
  tied to play/pause:

‚óŹ Done. Here's what was wrong and what I fixed.

  The bug

  The SOT Modal's waveform playhead bound to the raw DOM video element:
  :current-time="videoPlayerRef?.currentTime || 0"
  A native <video> element's currentTime property is not reactive ‚ÄĒ Vue has no
  way to know it's changing during playback, so the playhead sat frozen. It only
  "caught up" when some other state change forced a re-render (which is why it
  located the correct position on stop).

  The fix

  In the shared composable useTrimmableMediaModal.js:
  - Added a reactive currentTimeSec ref.
  - Added a requestAnimationFrame loop that updates it ~60fps while playing. (I
  used RAF rather than just the timeupdate event because timeupdate only fires
  ~4√ó/sec, which would make the playhead visibly stutter.)
  - The loop starts on @play and is cancelled on @pause and in onBeforeUnmount,
  with a final sync so the playhead lands exactly where playback stops.

  Then rebound both SotModal.vue and VoModal.vue (same composable, same bug) to
  :current-time="currentTimeSec".

  Seeks, frame-steps, and waveform-clicks were already covered for free ‚ÄĒ
  setting v.currentTime fires a native timeupdate, which syncs the ref.

  Verification

  - npm run lint --fix ‚Üí clean
  - Full vue-cli-service build ‚Üí green (the only warnings are pre-existing
  vendor postcss-calc ones; the first build failure was just an OOM during
  minify, fixed by bumping heap to 4096MB)
  - Only 3 files changed; I stayed clear of the TipTap editor lane the other
  session (63ebeb) is on, and coordinated on the relay before/after.

  One thing to note: this is the dev folder. After you're happy with it in dev,
  the same fix would need to land on main/live separately. Want me to do
  anything else, or were you also still wanting me to add those todo items you
  originally mentioned?

‚ÄĽ recap: Goal was to fix the SOT Modal playhead freezing during playback. That's done in the dev folder (3 files, lint and build clean). Next: test it in dev, then port the same fix to main/live. (disable recaps in /config)

‚óŹ Good, thorough scan. Let me verify the most material claim ‚ÄĒ whether there's a
  custom global undo manager (useUndoManager) and how it interacts with the
  ProseMirror history ‚ÄĒ since that determines whether undo is actually fine or

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ This confirms the key risk: there are two undo systems ‚ÄĒ a global
  useUndoManager (App.vue intercepts Ctrl+Z first) and ProseMirror's own
  UndoRedo extension. App.vue:527 only lets the global manager handle it if 
  undoManager.canUndo.value, otherwise it passes through to ProseMirror. That's
  the right precedence, but it's the exact spot where the two can fight (e.g. if
  both think they have undoable state). Let me write the todo.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Added as todo #34. The high-level scan turned up a clearer picture than "is it
  working?" ‚ÄĒ here's the verdict:

  Undo system: faring OK, with two real structural risks

  What's solid:
  - ProseMirror's UndoRedo extension is enabled (extensions.js:55).
  - All the new multi-select bulk ops (swap / join / delete-N / bullet /
  change-speaker) each commit as one transaction (BlockMultiSelect.js:247‚Äď365),
  so a single Ctrl+Z reverts the whole operation rather than unwinding it piece
  by piece. Drag-drop is also one transaction.
  - No direct DOM mutation that would bypass/corrupt the undo stack.

  Two things to watch (the meat of the todo):
  1. Two undo stacks coexist. There's a global useUndoManager composable and
  ProseMirror's own history. App.vue:523‚Äď535 grabs Ctrl+Z first and only
  forwards to the editor when undoManager.canUndo is false. The precedence is
  reasonable, but it's exactly where the two can fight ‚ÄĒ a stranded or double
  undo. Worth verifying.
  2. History clears on context switches. setContent() on item switch
  (ScriptEditor.vue:316) and on Code‚ÜĒScript mode roundtrips wipes the
  ProseMirror undo stack ‚ÄĒ so e.g. a Code-Mode edit can't be undone after
  returning to Script Mode. Probably intentional (per-item history), but it's
  undocumented and could look like a bug to a user.

  The todo includes a concrete manual-verification checklist for those. I set it
  normal priority ‚ÄĒ it's an audit/verify task, not a confirmed break.

  That's three todos added this session: #32 (waveform out-point marker), #33
  (autosave divergence, high), #34 (undo audit). Anything else?

  button, no inline per-section header. I filed it high since it's a

‚óŹ This is revealing for #32. The out-point marker IS rendered
  (AudioWaveform.vue:27), gated on outPoint !== null && duration > 0. The marker
  uses outPoint / duration for positioning ‚ÄĒ but recall duration in SotModal is
  bound to the non-reactive videoPlayerRef?.duration || 0 (the same reactivity
  bug I fixed for the playhead). So the out-marker style/region likely computes
  against duration = 0 until a re-render. Let me confirm the in-marker renders
  (user said in-point may show) vs out ‚ÄĒ and look at the out-point computation
  chain (outPointSeconds returns null when ‚Č§0).

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Now I understand #32 fully. Both in and out markers depend on props.duration,
  which in SotModal is the non-reactive videoPlayerRef?.duration || 0. The
  likely root cause is the same reactivity issue as the playhead ‚ÄĒ duration
  reads 0 (or stale) so outBracketStyle returns {} (no left), and the out marker
  either doesn't position or the region doesn't draw. The fix probably mirrors
  the playhead fix: expose a reactive durationSec from the composable.

‚óŹ This is the key finding. The two implementations use fundamentally different 
  drag mechanisms:

  - Rundown panel (RundownPanel.vue:215,320) uses the vuedraggable/SortableJS 
  library with :animation="200" ‚ÄĒ SortableJS's built-in FLIP-style animation
  that smoothly slides items as you drag.
  - Script editor (BlockDragHandle.js) is a custom ProseMirror pointer-based 
  plugin that fakes displacement via margin-top/margin-bottom CSS transitions
  (line 74-75, cubic-bezier). The user finds this "not very well animated."

  Let me look a bit more at the script editor's displacement animation to
  characterize the gap precisely.

‚óŹ Filed as #39 with a standalone plan, and updated the index.

  The grounding turned up the real reason it feels rough: the two drag systems 
  are completely different.

  - RundownPanel uses the vuedraggable/SortableJS library with :animation="200"
  ‚ÄĒ that's SortableJS's built-in FLIP animation (rows smoothly slide as you
  drag, item settles on drop). That's the smooth feel you're describing.
  - Script editor uses a custom ProseMirror pointer plugin (BlockDragHandle.js)
  that fakes the displacement by animating CSS margin-top/margin-bottom ‚ÄĒ which
  reflows the layout each frame and reads as snappier/jankier.

  The important caveat I baked into both the todo and the plan: you can't just 
  drop vuedraggable into the script editor. Its content is a ProseMirror
  document, not a Vue v-for list, so SortableJS can't own the DOM there. The
  work is to reproduce the rundown's smoothness inside the PM plugin ‚ÄĒ
  specifically by switching displacement from margin to GPU-composited
  transform: translateY, adding a proper FLIP settle on drop (measure rects
  before/after, animate the delta to zero ‚ÄĒ exactly what SortableJS does), and
  matching the ~200ms timing.

  ICR housekeeping: also done this turn ‚ÄĒ project name set to show-build, and I
  posted the identity-update renaming my sender from prefect-claude-746024 ‚Üí
  prefect-show-build-claude-746024.

  Running total: #32‚Äď#39 (eight todos), each with a detailed plan in
  /home/kevin/.claude/plans/show-build-todos/. No project files touched.
  Anything else?

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Three useful findings for the second pass:

  1. topLevelBlocks is duplicated in both BlockDragHandle.js:246 and
  BlockMultiSelect.js:74 ‚ÄĒ and #36 (speaker header) will need it a third time.
  This is a clear refactor-into-shared-util opportunity that should be sequenced
  before #36.
  2. There are 11 PM plugin files including CollapseSummary.js, LineNumbers.js,
  RevisionMark.js ‚ÄĒ these are existing examples of the decoration plugin pattern
  #36's SpeakerHeader.js should follow. Worth pointing the implementer at the
  closest analog.
  3. useUndoManager IS actively used by both App.vue AND ContentEditor.vue
  (which pushes to it at line¬†2063). So the two-stack concern in #34 is real,
  not theoretical ‚ÄĒ ContentEditor pushes operations onto the global manager
  while the PM editor maintains its own. Let me see what ContentEditor pushes,
  since that determines whether the stacks actually overlap.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Critical finding for #34. The global useUndoManager snapshots whole-script 
  content (scriptContent/scratchContent) and pushes coarse restore functions.
  The ProseMirror UndoRedo does fine-grained per-keystroke undo. So the two
  stacks operate at different granularities on the same content ‚ÄĒ this is
  exactly the kind of overlap that causes a Ctrl+Z to do the "wrong size" undo
  (revert a whole snapshot when the user expected one keystroke, or vice-versa).
  That materially sharpens #34's plan.

  Let me check one more thing ‚ÄĒ when these snapshots get captured (is it on
  script-mode edits, which would directly collide with PM's own history?).

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Let me see lines 1055-1075 to confirm which content watchers trigger the
  global undo capture ‚ÄĒ that determines whether the global stack overlaps PM's
  script-mode history.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ This is the decisive finding for #34. The rawMarkdownContent watcher (line
  1059) fires debouncedCaptureUndoState() for any content change ‚ÄĒ including
  script-mode edits, since PM edits flow up into rawMarkdownContent. So:

  - PM UndoRedo records the keystroke-level undo, and
  - the global useUndoManager records a coarse whole-script snapshot of the same
  edit.

  Both stacks now hold an entry for the same edit. App.vue checks the global
  manager first (undoManager.canUndo.value), so Ctrl+Z reverts the coarse global
  snapshot instead of the PM step ‚ÄĒ a real, reproducible conflict. This
  upgrades #34 from "audit/verify" to a "there's an actual bug here" finding,
  and it changes the recommended fix.

  I now have what the second pass needed. Let me write a consolidated
  "second-pass strategy" document and update the affected individual plans (#34
  especially, plus the #36/#39 shared-helper sequencing).

‚óŹ Second planning pass complete. Rather than just re-stating the per-todo plans,
  this pass dug into the code to find what only shows up when you look across
  all the todos together. Three findings actually changed the plans:

  Key findings from the deeper pass

  1. #34 is a real bug, not just an audit. I traced the undo path and confirmed:
  the rawMarkdownContent watcher (ContentEditor.vue:1059) captures a coarse 
  whole-script snapshot into the global useUndoManager on every Script-Mode edit
  ‚ÄĒ at the same time ProseMirror's UndoRedo records the same edit
  per-keystroke. Since App.vue:527 checks the global manager first, Ctrl+Z in 
  Script Mode reverts the wrong granularity (a whole snapshot instead of one
  step). The global manager's own comment claims it's scoped to Code/Scratch
  only ‚ÄĒ but the watcher betrays that. I rewrote #34 from "verify & document" to
  a concrete fix: scope the global manager out of Script Mode and let PM own
  Ctrl+Z there.

  2. A shared blockUtils.js extraction should come before #36. topLevelBlocks()
  is already copy-pasted in both BlockDragHandle.js and BlockMultiSelect.js, and
  #36 (speaker header) would make it a third copy. Extracting it first removes
  drift and unblocks #36 cleanly. I also pointed the implementer at
  LineNumbers.js/CollapseSummary.js as the existing decoration-plugin pattern to
  mirror.

  3. #32 and the playhead fix are the same reactivity class ‚ÄĒ batch them.

  The cross-cutting insight: ordering matters

  The big new artifact is PASS-2-STRATEGY.md, which adds what per-todo plans
  can't:

  - A dependency graph ‚ÄĒ the headline being that #34 must be settled before you 
  can meaningfully verify #38 and #39, because both produce PM transactions
  whose undo is muddied by the double-capture bug.
  - The editor-lane serialization constraint: five todos (#34, #36, #38, #39, +
  the refactor) all touch files owned by the active session 63ebeb ‚ÄĒ they can't 
  be parallelized; they need one claimed handoff window, done in series.
  - Three suggested batches: (1) quick wins with no lane contention ‚ÄĒ #37, #32,
  #35; (2) the #33 investigation block; (3) the serialized editor-lane sequence.
  - A risk register and four product questions worth batching to you before any
  coding (e.g. #35 preview-vs-diff, #36 whether to change the default-Josh).

  All in /home/kevin/.claude/plans/show-build-todos/ ‚ÄĒ index + 8 per-todo plans
  + the pass-2 strategy. Still zero project files touched.

  Those four open product questions are the natural next step ‚ÄĒ want me to put
  them to you now so the plans are decision-complete?

‚óŹ This is the "capture initial snapshot after load" call. This one is harmless 
  to leave ‚ÄĒ it captures a baseline of the loaded state. The bug is the per-edit
  capture in Script Mode, which I've gated. The post-load baseline doesn't
  accumulate per keystroke. But to keep the global manager truly empty in Script
  Mode (so App.vue's undoManager.canUndo is false and Ctrl+Z always falls
  through to PM), I should gate this too ‚ÄĒ otherwise a single baseline entry
  could still make canUndo true. Let me gate it for consistency.

‚óŹ Now there's a subtlety: when the user switches from Code/Scratch mode to
  Script mode, the global manager may already hold entries (making canUndo
  true), so App.vue would still steal Ctrl+Z in Script mode. The robust fix is
  in App.vue itself: prefer the focused PM editor. Let me check App.vue's
  handler to add a "PM editor focused" guard.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ There's already an isInNativeTextField(e.target) early-return (line 526). The
  cleanest robust guard: if the Ctrl+Z originates from inside the ProseMirror
  editor, let PM handle it and don't let the global manager fire. ProseMirror's
  editable DOM has class .ProseMirror. Let me add that check alongside the
  native-text-field guard.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Important ‚ÄĒ this comment reveals the legacy design assumption: it says
  script-mode contenteditables carry .script-paragraph and "belong to our undo
  manager." That was true for the old contenteditable editor. With the
  ProseMirror editor, the editable element is .ProseMirror and PM owns undo. So
  the legacy logic deliberately routed .script-paragraph to the global manager ‚ÄĒ
  but the PM editor isn't .script-paragraph.

  The correct fix: treat the ProseMirror editor as a native-undo zone (let PM
  handle Ctrl+Z). Let me update isInNativeTextField to also exclude/return-true
  for .ProseMirror.

‚óŹ Update(disaffected-ui/src/App.vue)
  ‚éŅ ¬†Added 6 lines, removed 4 lines
      504  const undoManager = useUndoManager()
      505  
      506  // True when the keyboard target is a real text-entry element 
           where the
      507 -// browser's native undo should win (sidebar fields, modal inp
          -uts, etc).                                                    
      508 -// Script-mode paragraph contenteditables are excluded ‚ÄĒ they 
          -carry the                                                     
      509 -// `script-paragraph` class and belong to our undo manager bec
          -ause the                                                      
      510 -// reactive `rawMarkdownContent` is the source of truth, not t
          -he DOM.                                                       
      507 +// browser's native (or editor-owned) undo should win (sidebar
          + fields, modal                                                
      508 +// inputs, etc) ‚ÄĒ in those cases the global useUndoManager mus
          +t NOT fire.                                                   
      509 +// The ProseMirror script editor (.ProseMirror) is included: i
          +t owns Ctrl+Z                                                 
      510 +// with its own fine-grained history, so a Ctrl+Z inside it mu
          +st reach PM and                                               
      511 +// never be intercepted by the global manager (todo #34).     
      512  function isInNativeTextField(target) {
      513    if (!target || !(target instanceof Element)) return false
      514 +  if (target.closest('.ProseMirror')) return true             
      515    return !!target.closest('input, textarea, [contenteditable="
           true"]:not(.script-paragraph)')
      516  }
      517  

‚óŹ This App.vue guard is the robust fix ‚ÄĒ regardless of whether the global
  manager has stale entries from a prior Code-Mode session, a Ctrl+Z fired while
  focused in the PM editor now always reaches PM. The ContentEditor gating I
  added prevents the global manager from accumulating Script-Mode entries in the
  first place. Together they fully resolve the two-stack conflict.

‚ĚĮ can the version previewerbe set to preview with the same rendering as script

‚óŹ Good call ‚ÄĒ raw markdown in a <pre> isn't friendly for most users. Let me do
  the ICR check-in, then figure out how Script Mode renders so the preview can
  reuse it.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ ICR check-in: nothing actionable ‚ÄĒ only my own messages on the relay.

  Now the design question. Script Mode renders via TipTap/ProseMirror:
  markdownToDoc() parses the markdown into a PM document, and the ScriptEditor
  component renders it as the visual editor. The cleanest way to make the
  preview look like Script Mode is to mount a read-only ScriptEditor with the
  version's content, rather than a <pre>.

  Let me check whether ScriptEditor supports a read-only/editable=false mode,
  since that determines the approach.

  Read 1 file, ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ ScriptEditor doesn't currently expose editable. Reusing the full ScriptEditor
  for preview is overkill and risky (it has cue modals, slash menus, save logic,
  multi-select). For a clean read-only Script-Mode-looking render, the lighter
  approach is a small self-contained preview that mounts its own read-only
  ProseMirror Editor using the same buildScriptExtensions + markdownToDoc, with
  editable: false. This reuses the exact rendering (paragraphs, cue cards,
  speaker styling) without dragging in all the editing machinery.

  Let me look at how Script Mode styling is scoped and confirm
  buildScriptExtensions is importable standalone, then decide between (a) a tiny
  dedicated read-only viewer component vs (b) adding an editable prop to
  ScriptEditor.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ buildScriptExtensions is cleanly importable. But there's a catch:
  ScriptEditor's CSS is <style scoped> (line 485) ‚ÄĒ so a separate viewer
  component wouldn't get those styles, and the cue cards render via a Vue
  NodeView (CueNodeView.vue) that brings its own styling. The paragraph/speaker
  styling, though, lives in ScriptEditor's scoped CSS.

  Given the scoped-CSS issue, the cleanest and lowest-risk path is to add an
  editable prop to ScriptEditor itself and reuse it in read-only mode for the
  preview ‚ÄĒ it already has all the correct styling and the cue NodeView wiring.
  Let me verify what editable: false would need (disable save/onUpdate, skip the
  cue/multi-select callbacks). Let me check the build-extensions signature and
  the watchers that might fire in read-only mode.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ I have a clear picture. Reusing the full ScriptEditor would also wire up cue
  edit/delete modals in a preview, which we don't want. The right scope: a
  lightweight read-only preview that mounts a ProseMirror editor with editable: 
  false and the same extensions, but inside a component that shares the visual
  styling.

  Given the scoped-CSS constraint, the safest approach that guarantees identical
  rendering is: make ScriptEditor accept an editable prop (default true), and
  when false: set editable: false on the Editor, and no-op the cue/save
  callbacks. Then the preview dialog renders a real <ScriptEditor 
  :script-content="versionPreviewContent" :editable="false" />. That reuses 100%
  of the styling and cue rendering with zero duplication.

  Let me implement. First, add the editable prop and apply it in the Editor
  config.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ The cue cards have their own edit/delete buttons. Gating all of that for
  read-only would be a deeper change touching the NodeView and card components ‚ÄĒ
  more than this preview warrants.

  The pragmatic approach: the preview's main value is rendering paragraphs,
  speakers, and cue cards visually like Script Mode (vs raw markdown). The cue
  cards being non-functional in a preview is acceptable since editable: false
  already makes the buttons no-op (onUpdate gated, save gated, cue callbacks
  just emit up but the preview's ScriptEditor isn't wired to any modal). But to
  be safe and clean, I'll make CueNodeView read editor.isEditable and hide the
  action buttons via a readonly prop on the cards.

  Actually ‚ÄĒ let me reconsider scope. The cards read editor.isEditable is the
  clean signal. Let me check if PlaceholderCueCard already accepts a
  readonly/collapsed-style prop I can leverage, to avoid a sprawling change.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ No existing readonly prop on the cards. Gating every cue card's buttons would
  be a sizable change spanning the NodeView and multiple card components ‚ÄĒ
  disproportionate for a preview dialog.

  Better engineering decision: keep the preview simple and self-contained.
  Render the version with Script-Mode visual styling, but pass the version
  content through and rely on editable: false to prevent edits. The cue cards'
  edit/delete buttons emit events up to the preview's ScriptEditor, which I will
  simply not wire to any handler (no @edit-cue/@delete-cue listeners) ‚ÄĒ so
  clicks are harmless no-ops. That's the minimal, safe path and gives you
  exactly what you asked: Script-Mode rendering instead of code.

  Read 1 file, ran 2 shell commands (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ There's a watcher on scriptContent (line 326) that calls loadContent(next)
  when the prop changes. So even though the <ScriptEditor> mounts with empty
  content and versionPreviewContent arrives async, the watcher will load it once
  it updates. The :key="versionPreviewNumber" ensures switching versions
  remounts cleanly. Good ‚ÄĒ this works.

  Now I should also reconsider the dialog data flow: I set showVersionPreview = 
  true before the fetch, so ScriptEditor mounts with empty content, then the
  watcher fills it. That's fine. Let me update the old .version-preview-content
  CSS (now unused) ‚ÄĒ replace with a wrapper style. Let me lint first to catch
  issues, then adjust CSS.

‚óŹ Done (1afff32). The version preview now renders in Script Mode visuals instead
  of raw code.

  What changed

  - Added an editable prop to ScriptEditor (defaults to true, so nothing else
  changes). When false, it mounts the ProseMirror editor with editable: false
  and skips saves ‚ÄĒ same component, same styling, just non-interactive.
  - The preview dialog now renders <ScriptEditor :editable="false"> instead of
  the <pre> block, so you see paragraphs, speaker styling, and cue cards exactly
  like the live Script Mode editor ‚ÄĒ parsed from the same markdownToDoc
  pipeline.
  - Keyed on the version number so switching between versions remounts cleanly.

  Why this approach: I reused the real ScriptEditor rather than building a
  separate viewer, because its visual styling is scoped and the cue cards render
  through a Vue NodeView ‚ÄĒ duplicating that would have drifted from the real
  thing. Reusing it guarantees the preview matches Script Mode pixel-for-pixel.

  One honest caveat: the cue cards in the preview still show their edit/delete
  buttons (gating those per-card would've been a sprawling change across the
  NodeView + every card type). But they're harmless ‚ÄĒ editable: false plus the
  fact that I deliberately wired no edit/delete listeners to the preview's
  ScriptEditor means clicking them does nothing. If you'd rather those buttons
  be hidden in preview, that's a follow-up I can scope ‚ÄĒ just say so.

  Lint + full build green; committed as a follow-up to #35 (so it's still
  isolated/revertable).

  ICR check-in done earlier this turn ‚ÄĒ nothing actionable.

  Want me to fold this "hide cue buttons in read-only" into a quick follow-up,
  or move on to the #33 investigation (diagnose-only)?

‚óŹ As I suspected ‚ÄĒ the child components use different classes: .fsq-delete-btn,
  .gfx-action-btn, and the FSQ edit button at line 66 has no distinct class.
  This is getting into whack-a-mole territory across many components, and
  CSS-hiding individual buttons is fragile (new buttons would leak through).

  A cleaner, more robust solution: since the whole preview is non-interactive,
  disable pointer events on the entire cue card in the preview, so none of its
  buttons (current or future) are clickable ‚ÄĒ while keeping it fully
  visible/styled. That's one rule that covers everything. But that also kills
  the collapse toggle and scrolling within cards.

  Better still: the buttons should be hidden at the source based on a real
  readonly signal, not CSS. Let me reconsider ‚ÄĒ the most correct fix is to gate
  on editor.isEditable in CueNodeView, passing a readonly prop into the cards.
  Let me check how many card components need it and whether it's tractable. The
  cards are: PlaceholderCueCard, ImageCueCard, and the cue-type children. Let me
  look at CueNodeView's access to the editor and whether I can pass :readonly.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ CueNodeView has editor via nodeViewProps, so this.editor.isEditable is
  available. The clean fix: pass :readonly="!editor.isEditable" from CueNodeView
  into both cards, and have the cards gate their action buttons with
  v-if="!readonly". The cue-type children inherit it via the parent passing it
  down.

  But that's edits across CueNodeView + PlaceholderCueCard + ImageCueCard +
  FsqCueContent + GfxCueContent ‚ÄĒ 5 files. That IS the correct, durable fix
  though. The CSS approach I started is fragile (just proved it misses child
  classes).

  Let me go with the robust readonly-prop approach but keep it tight. First,
  revert the fragile CSS to just the cosmetic bits (cursor/drag-gutter), and
  remove the button-hiding CSS since the prop will handle it.

‚óŹ Done (81d49d3). The version preview now reads as a true read-only view.

  What I did

  Threaded a readonly prop from CueNodeView (driven by editor.isEditable) down
  into all the cue card components, gating their action buttons with
  v-if="!readonly":

  - CueNodeView ‚Üí passes :readonly="!editor.isEditable" to both card types
  - PlaceholderCueCard ‚Üí hides the edit/delete .cue-actions (RIF + non-RIF)
  - ImageCueCard ‚Üí hides its edit/delete actions
  - FsqCueContent ‚Üí hides "Edit FSQ" + delete
  - GfxCueContent ‚Üí hides the generate/download/edit icon group + delete
  - Plus the drag gutter is hidden in the preview wrapper (reordering is off
  too)

  Why the prop instead of CSS: I started with a CSS-hide approach but found the
  child components use different button classes (.fsq-delete-btn,
  .gfx-action-btn, unclassed FSQ edit) ‚ÄĒ CSS would've been whack-a-mole and
  would leak any future buttons. The readonly prop is the durable fix: it's
  driven by the real editor.isEditable signal, so it also correctly does nothing
  in the normal editable editor.

  Lint + full build green. Committed as another isolated follow-up to #35.

‚ĚĮ describe #33 and begin investigation                                          

‚óŹ What #33 is

  The symptom you saw: editing episode 0276, a red banner appeared ‚ÄĒ "Autosave 
  declined ‚ÄĒ server has MORE content (1372 chars) than your local copy (382). 
  Your edits are NOT saved. Reload to sync, or press Ctrl+S to overwrite the 
  server." Autosave silently refused to save.

  The key reframe (from my earlier pass): that banner comes from a deliberate 
  safety guard, and the guard is working correctly. It's refusing to let a
  382-char local copy overwrite a 1372-char server copy ‚ÄĒ exactly the data-loss
  protection we want. So #33 is not "fix the guard."

  The real bug to find: why did your local editor end up holding only 382 chars 
  when the server had 1372? A healthy editor shouldn't diverge to a truncated
  copy without a reload. My hypothesis going in is the known silent-freeze
  failure mode ‚ÄĒ a load that stalls or aborts (isLoadingItemContent stuck, or a
  probe abort) leaving the editor with stale/partial content while the DB moved
  ahead.

  This is investigate-only per your instruction ‚ÄĒ I'll diagnose and report, and
  not change any code until you say go.

  Let me begin. First, the guard itself and the load path.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ This is informative. There are multiple guards stacked in saveCurrentItem:
  1. Line 3586 ‚ÄĒ skip autosave while isLoadingItemContent (cross-item bleed
  protection)
  2. Line 3617-3621 ‚ÄĒ block save if multiple YAML frontmatter blocks
  3. Line 3589 (the remote probe I saw earlier) ‚ÄĒ the divergence guard that
  fired your banner

  The fact that the remote-probe guard fired means guard #1
  (isLoadingItemContent) did not block it ‚ÄĒ so at save time isLoadingItemContent
  was false, yet the content was still truncated (382 chars). That points away
  from "stuck mid-load" and toward "a load completed but produced truncated 
  content," or "the buffer got reset after load."

  Let me trace the load path ‚ÄĒ how rawMarkdownContent gets populated and where
  isLoadingItemContent toggles.

‚ĚĮ are these gaurds artifacts of the old editor? 

‚óŹ Great question, and it cuts to the heart of #33. Let me answer it directly,
  because the answer changes how we should think about the whole bug.

  Are these guards artifacts of the old editor?

  Yes ‚ÄĒ substantially. Look at what loadItemContent and its guards are defending
  against. The comments name the specific incidents:

  - The isLoadingItemContent guard (line 4172-4176) exists because of "the 
  2026-05-27 ep0275 'saves froze for 4.5h, no error shown' bug" and "the same 
  cue appeared in every item corruption (a frozen editor left rawMarkdownContent
  stuck, and each switch saved it onto the next item)."
  - The frontmatter-stripping loop (4389-4418) cleans "legacy/corrupted data."
  - The removed duplicate-detection (4424-4428) "was causing false positives and
  permanently truncating legitimate content... The autosave watcher would then 
  save the truncated version back to the DB."

  All of these describe the old contenteditable architecture's central hazard:
  rawMarkdownContent was a single shared reactive string that the visual editor,
  the autosave watcher, and item-switching all fought over. A frozen or stale
  rawMarkdownContent was the root of every one of those corruption incidents.
  The guards are scar tissue from that fight.

  Here's the critical architectural point for #33: In the new ProseMirror world,
  the script editor (ScriptEditor.vue) owns its own ProseMirror document as the
  source of truth and serializes out to rawMarkdownContent. The old
  loadItemContent ‚Üí loadRawMarkdownContent ‚Üí rawMarkdownContent flow still
  exists and still feeds the system, but rawMarkdownContent is no longer the
  editing surface ‚ÄĒ it's a serialization sink/source bridging to the DB.

  So there are really two possibilities for your 0276 banner, and they have
  opposite fixes:

  1. The guards are now partly vestigial and one of them (or the stale
  item.script they read from) is producing the truncated 382-char copy ‚ÄĒ in
  which case the fix is to simplify/retire the old path and load fresh from the
  DB.
  2. The guards are still load-bearing because the new editor still routes
  through rawMarkdownContent, and the truncation is a real stale-load ‚ÄĒ in which
  case the fix is to harden the load.

  Let me verify which by checking the one thing that decides it: does 
  loadItemContent ever fetch fresh from the DB, or does it only ever read the 
  in-memory item.script? And where does item.script in rundownItems get its
  value ‚ÄĒ a full episode load, or incremental updates?

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ There's a per-item refetch mechanism ‚ÄĒ line 3472
  rundown-item-by-id/${syncDbId} and a selectRundownItem at 4586. Let me read
  selectRundownItem (4586-4721) to see whether item-switch refetches fresh
  content from DB or loads the in-memory item. This decides the whole diagnosis.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ This is the smoking gun for the diagnosis. selectRundownItem loads 
  this.rundownItems[index] ‚ÄĒ the in-memory copy ‚ÄĒ never a fresh DB fetch (line
  4719-4721). And the whole save-before-switch dance (STEP 1-3, lines 4616-4647)
  exists to keep that in-memory .script current. There's a heavily-commented
  warning right there at line 4616-4619:

  ‚Ėé "STEP 1: Clear editing flags BEFORE flush/save so the save can update 
  ‚Ėé rundownItems[].script... Without this, the mutation is skipped and the 
  ‚Ėé item's local .script stays stale, causing data loss when switching back."

  So the divergence chain for your 0276 banner is now clear. Let me confirm the
  final link ‚ÄĒ the post-save write-back at line 3699 (savedItem.script = 
  contentToSave) and whether it's gated by isActivelyEditing.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ I have the complete diagnosis. Let me update the #33 plan file with findings
  (I'm allowed to write plan files; not touching project code), then give you
  the full picture.

‚óŹ Updated plan
  ‚éŅ ¬†/plan to preview

‚óŹ Answering your question, then the diagnosis

  Are these guards artifacts of the old editor? Yes ‚ÄĒ substantially. Every guard
  in the load path is scar tissue from the old contenteditable architecture,
  where rawMarkdownContent was the actual editing surface that three things
  fought over (the visual editor, the autosave watcher, item-switching). The
  code comments literally name the incidents:
  - isLoadingItemContent guard ‚Üí the "ep0275 saves-froze-4.5h" bug
  - the editing-flag clearing ‚Üí the "same cue appeared in every item" corruption
  - a removed duplicate-detector ‚Üí "permanently truncating legitimate content"
  then autosaving the truncation

  All old-editor hazards.

  What's actually causing the 0276 banner

  I traced the full chain. The editor never re-fetches an item's content from 
  the DB when you select it. selectRundownItem(index) loads
  this.rundownItems[index] ‚ÄĒ the in-memory copy ‚ÄĒ and loadRawMarkdownContent
  just reads item.script. The only thing keeping that in-memory .script current
  is:
  - the save-before-switch dance (steps 1-3 in selectRundownItem), and
  - the post-save write-back savedItem.script = contentToSave.

  So 382 chars means rundownItems[i].script was stale/truncated in memory ‚ÄĒ the
  editor faithfully loaded a copy that was already behind the DB. The
  remote-probe guard then correctly caught it and refused to clobber the
  1372-char server copy. The banner is the safety net working; the bug is the 
  stale in-memory copy upstream.

  The architectural reframe (this is the important part)

  In the new ProseMirror world, ScriptEditor.vue owns its PM document as the
  source of truth and serializes out to rawMarkdownContent. The old item.script
  ‚Üí rawMarkdownContent path is now just a bridge to the DB, not the editing
  surface ‚ÄĒ yet item-switch still trusts the in-memory .script instead of the 
  DB. That's the seam where staleness creeps in.

  The durable fix is almost certainly to re-fetch the item fresh from the DB on 
  select (the GET /api/episodes/rundown-item-by-id/{db_id} endpoint already
  exists ‚ÄĒ the probe uses it). That would eliminate the entire "stale in-memory
  copy" class of bug and let us retire several of these now-vestigial guards.

  I'm pausing here per your instruction ‚ÄĒ no code changed. Full findings written
  to todo-33-autosave-divergence.md.

  Two things would sharpen the fix before I touch anything:

  1. Can you tell me how 0276 got into that state? (e.g. did you restore a
  version, have a second tab open, or leave it idle a long time?) That
  distinguishes "a save silently failed earlier" from "a legit stale-tab the
  guard correctly blocked."
  2. I can do read-only DB forensics on the live showbuild DB ‚ÄĒ check 0276's
  current script_content length and its ContentVersion history to see whether
  the 1372-char server copy came from a normal save, a restore, or another path.
  Want me to run that?

‚ĚĮ do you think simply removing these vestigial artifacts is the right move?     

‚óŹ Short answer: no ‚ÄĒ not "simply" removing them, not yet. Let me be precise
  about why, because the distinction matters for not re-introducing a 4.5-hour
  data-loss bug.

  The guards are vestigial, but they're not inert

  Here's the trap: these guards are artifacts of the old editor's editing 
  surface, but the data path they sit on ‚ÄĒ item.script (in-memory) ‚Üí
  loadItemContent ‚Üí rawMarkdownContent ‚Üí autosave ‚Üí DB ‚ÄĒ is still live and still
  load-bearing. The new ProseMirror editor didn't replace that path; it sits on
  top of it and serializes into it.

  So "simply removing the guards" would strip the safety net off a pipe that
  still carries the stale-in-memory-copy hazard. You'd very likely reproduce
  exactly the corruption the comments describe ‚ÄĒ just without the guard that
  currently catches it (the banner you saw is that guard working).

  The right move is to remove the cause, then the guards follow

  The guards exist because item-switch trusts a stale in-memory .script instead 
  of the DB. Fix that root cause ‚ÄĒ re-fetch the item's content fresh from the DB
  on select ‚ÄĒ and the guards don't need removing one-by-one; most of them
  become genuinely unreachable, and you delete them as a consequence, with
  confidence, because the condition they guard against can no longer occur.

  That's the difference between:
  - ‚ĚĆ "delete the guards" ‚Üí removing detection while the disease remains
  - ‚úÖ "remove the staleness, then the guards are dead code" ‚Üí removing the
  disease, detection retires itself

  The order is the whole point. Remove cause ‚Üí verify the guards now never fire
  ‚Üí then delete them.

  But there's a real question underneath your question

  There's a legitimate case that the even bigger vestigial thing is the entire
  item.script ‚Üí rawMarkdownContent in-memory bridge itself. If ScriptEditor owns
  the document and the DB is the source of truth, you arguably want item-switch
  to load from DB and save to DB, and stop caching .script in rundownItems for
  content at all (keep it only for the rundown panel's flag detection). That's a
  more thorough refactor that would retire the staleness and the guards and the
  bridge ‚ÄĒ but it's bigger and riskier, and it's #33's territory, not a free
  side-quest.

  So my recommendation, in order of increasing ambition:

‚óŹ¬†User answered Claude's questions:
  ‚éŅ ¬†¬∑ Given the guards are vestigial but the stale-in-memory data path under 
     them is still live, how far should the #33 fix go? ‚Üí full investigation, 
     simulation, and plan to fix this in the most robust way possible

‚óŹ Understood ‚ÄĒ full investigation, simulation, and a plan for the most robust
  fix. Still no production code changes; this is diagnose + prove + design.

  Let me run the three strands in parallel: (1) read-only DB forensics on 0276
  to see exactly how it diverged, (2) map every code path that writes/reads the
  in-memory .script so the "simulation" is grounded in reality, and (3) confirm
  the backend endpoints available for a fetch-on-select design.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

  current DB value is 383, and updated_at is 2026-05-31 ‚ÄĒ older than item 1140

  Wait ‚ÄĒ your banner said the server had 1372 and local had 382. The "Next
  Block" item is currently 383 in the DB. So the banner fired when the server 
  already had a longer copy (1372) and your local was 382 ‚ÄĒ meaning at that
  moment the server was ahead. But now the DB shows 383 for that slug. Let me
  check the version history for the 382/1372 item to reconstruct the timeline.

  versions for both candidate items.

‚óŹ This is the bug, captured in the version history. Look at item 1144's timeline
  on 2026-05-31 at ~02:02-02:03:

  v35  569  manual_save  josh   02:02:08
  v36   48  autosave     josh   02:02:10   ‚Üź 2s later, autosave DROPS to 48
  chars
  v37  569  restore      kevin  02:02:43   ‚Üź kevin restores back to 569
  v38  541  autosave     kevin  02:02:56
  v39  585  manual_save  kevin  02:03:10
  v40  395  autosave     kevin  02:03:28   ‚Üź drops to 395

‚óŹ Investigation complete, with hard evidence. Here's what I found ‚ÄĒ and it
  changes the picture from "stale tab" to something more serious.

  The bug, caught red-handed in the DB

  I pulled episode 0276's version history. Item 1144 ("Next Block") thrashed 
  between two sessions on 2026-05-31:

  v35  569  manual_save  josh    02:02:08
  v36   48  autosave     josh    02:02:10   ‚Üź his OWN editor autosaved a stale
  48-char buffer 2s later
  v37  569  restore      kevin   02:02:43   ‚Üź kevin had to RESTORE the lost
  content
  v40  395  autosave     kevin   02:03:28   ‚Üź drops again
  v42  383  autosave     josh    02:03:44   ‚Üź settles at 383 (the "382" in your
  banner)

  That v36 ‚ÄĒ josh's editor dropping 569 chars to 48 two seconds after his own 
  save ‚ÄĒ is the data-loss event, captured. Your 382/1372 banner was the guard
  catching a later instance of the same thing.

  Root cause is two compounding failures

  1. The save endpoint is pure last-write-wins. PUT /save-rundown does
  script_content = new_script with no version check, no hash compare, no lock 
  enforcement (rundown_router.py:772). Whatever the client sends wins.
  2. The client edits a stale in-memory copy. Item-switch loads
  rundownItems[index].script ‚ÄĒ never re-fetches from DB. When that in-memory
  copy goes stale (a skipped save, or another session/restore moving the DB),
  autosave pushes the stale copy over fresher server content.

  So: stale client copy + a server that accepts anything = silent data loss. The
  guards you asked about are old-editor scar tissue sitting on top of this
  still-live hazard ‚ÄĒ which is exactly why "just delete them" is wrong: you'd
  remove the detection and keep the disease.

  To answer your earlier question definitively

  No, removing the vestigial guards is not the right move ‚ÄĒ not on its own. The
  correct order is: kill the cause (stale copy + blind overwrite), verify the
  guards now never fire, then delete them as dead code.

  The robust fix (simulated to prove it works)

  Full design is in todo-33-FULL-INVESTIGATION.md. Layered, in order:
  - A. Server optimistic concurrency ‚ÄĒ client sends the content_hash it started
  from; server returns 409 on mismatch instead of overwriting. This is the one 
  change that makes silent last-write-wins impossible regardless of client bugs.
  - B. Fetch-on-select ‚ÄĒ load fresh from DB on item switch, killing the stale
  base.
  - C. Client handles 409 gracefully (reload-theirs / keep-mine).
  - D. Retire the vestigial guards ‚ÄĒ last, as a consequence, one at a time.

  I replayed the v35‚Üív36 sequence against this design: with Layer A, josh's
  stale autosave gets a 409 and is rejected, the editor re-syncs from DB, and
  v36 never happens.

  Three decisions I need from you before any code (in the plan, but the big
  one):

  Is concurrent multi-user editing of the same segment a workflow you want to 
  support (‚Üí invest in merge/conflict UX), or should it be single-writer locked
  (‚Üí enforce the existing SegmentLock harder and make the second editor
  read-only)? That fork changes the whole fix shape ‚ÄĒ everything else (hash
  token, 409 default) I can recommend defaults for.

  Still zero production code touched. How do you want to answer that fork?

‚ĚĮ yes,  we do want to support multiple users editing the same document at the

‚óŹ That's a significant decision ‚ÄĒ and an honest one deserves a clear-eyed
  response, because "Google Docs-style" is a much bigger thing than the #33 bug 
  fix, and I don't want to quietly let #33 balloon into a multi-week project
  without you choosing that deliberately.

  Let me lay out what you're actually asking for, because there's a spectrum
  here.

  What "like Google Docs" really means

  Real-time collaborative editing (multiple cursors, character-by-character
  merge, no save button, no conflicts ever) requires CRDT or Operational 
  Transform infrastructure. For a TipTap/ProseMirror app the standard path is
  Yjs (y-prosemirror + a y-websocket server). That's genuinely achievable here ‚ÄĒ
  ProseMirror has first-class Yjs support ‚ÄĒ but it's a real build:

  - A WebSocket sync server (new backend service + deployment)
  - Yjs document state per segment, with persistence/snapshotting to Postgres
  - Awareness layer (live cursors/presence)
  - Reconciling Yjs as the live source of truth with your existing
  script_content/version-history/markdown-serialization model
  - The autosave/save-rundown path largely gets replaced, not patched

  That's weeks, and it touches the editor's core ‚ÄĒ the exact code session 63ebeb
  just migrated. It's the right end-state if collaboration is a product pillar,
  but it is not the #33 fix.

  The key insight: these are two different deliverables

  #33 is a data-loss bug. It needs to be safe now, this week. Google-Docs
  collaboration is a feature you want eventually. The worst outcome is letting
  the feature ambition delay the bug fix, leaving real data loss in production
  while the big thing gets built.

  So I'd strongly recommend decoupling them: ship the #33 safety fix immediately
  (so no one loses work), and plan the real-time collaboration as its own
  initiative. The good news is the #33 fix I designed ‚ÄĒ server-side conflict
  detection (409) + fetch-on-select ‚ÄĒ is not throwaway; it's the correct safety
  floor and a stepping stone, and it's compatible with a later Yjs migration.

  Let me get the scope decision crisp:

‚óŹ¬†User declined to answer questions
  ‚éŅ ¬†¬∑ Given you want Google-Docs-style collaboration, how should we sequence it
     against the #33 data-loss bug? (Fix #33 now, Yjs later / Skip straight to 
     Yjs / Interim lock, then Yjs)

‚ĚĮ if we were tpo move straight into the google docs style multiuser edit. would 
  that make the dataloss bug disapear by default?                               

‚óŹ Mostly yes ‚ÄĒ but with two important asterisks. Let me be precise, because "the
  bug disappears by default" is the kind of claim that's 90% true and the
  missing 10% is where you lose data anyway.

  Why it largely disappears

  The #33 bug is fundamentally last-write-wins on a whole-document blob. Two
  editors each hold a full copy of the script; whoever saves last overwrites the
  other, and a stale copy can clobber a fresh one. That entire failure mode is
  structural ‚ÄĒ it exists because the unit of save is "the entire script_content
  string."

  Google-Docs-style editing (Yjs/CRDT) removes that structure entirely. There is
  no "save my whole copy over yours." Instead, each keystroke is a small
  operation ("insert 'x' at position 12"), and the CRDT merges everyone's
  operations deterministically. Two people editing different sentences both
  land. Two people editing the same word merge to a defined result instead of
  one erasing the other. There's no stale full-document blob to overwrite
  anything with. So the v36 event ‚ÄĒ josh's editor dropping 569 chars to 48 ‚ÄĒ
  cannot happen by construction. That's real, and it's the strongest argument
  for going straight to Yjs.

  The two asterisks (where it does NOT disappear by default)

  1. The persistence boundary. Yjs keeps the live document in memory / in the
  WebSocket server. At some point it must be written to Postgres
  (script_content) and serialized to your markdown + version history. That 
  write-back is a fresh last-write-wins surface unless you build it carefully.
  If the Yjs‚ÜíPostgres snapshot logic is naive ‚ÄĒ or if the markdown serialization
  runs concurrently with edits ‚ÄĒ you can reintroduce a clobber at the save 
  layer even though the live editing layer is conflict-free. So the bug doesn't
  vanish; it moves to the persistence seam, and you have to get that seam right.
  This is the single most common way teams "adopt CRDT and still lose data."

  2. The migration window is the dangerous part. Right now the editor is a
  brand-new ProseMirror migration that session 63ebeb just finished. Ripping out
  the autosave/save-rundown path and replacing it with Yjs is major surgery on
  freshly-laid code. During that build ‚ÄĒ which is weeks ‚ÄĒ the current data-loss 
  bug is still live in production. So "go straight to Yjs" doesn't make the bug
  disappear today; it makes it disappear whenever Yjs ships, and leaves real
  users losing work in the interval.

  The honest bottom line

  - Does Yjs eliminate the class of bug? Yes ‚ÄĒ the document-blob last-write-wins
  race is designed out.
  - Does it eliminate it "by default / for free"? No. You still have to build a
  correct CRDT-to-Postgres persistence layer, or you recreate the same clobber
  one layer down.
  - Does it fix it now? No. It's a multi-week build; the bug bleeds until it
  lands.

  That's exactly why I keep separating the two: the #33 server-side conflict
  check (the 409) isn't throwaway work even if you go full Yjs ‚ÄĒ it's precisely 
  the guard you want protecting the Yjs‚ÜíPostgres persistence boundary, the one
  place Yjs doesn't save you. So the interim fix and the end-goal aren't
  competing; the interim fix is a component of doing Yjs safely.

  My recommendation stands: ship the small #33 safety fix now (stops the
  bleeding this week, and it's reusable as the persistence guard), then build
  Yjs as its own initiative. But I want to hear how you weigh "stop the loss
  this week" against "just go build the real thing" ‚ÄĒ and whether the multi-week
  exposure window is acceptable to you. Which way do you want to go?

‚ĚĮ /tts

‚óŹ Let me do the ICR check-in, then give you a real answer on the persistence
  layer ‚ÄĒ this is the crux of doing Yjs safely.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ ICR check-in: nothing new actionable ‚ÄĒ my clear-to-push reply is the latest;
  1ed2f6 hasn't responded since. Proceeding.

  Now the real question ‚ÄĒ how do you save at the persistence layer with Yjs?
  This is the part everyone gets wrong, so let me give you the actual mechanics,
  not hand-waving.

  The core mental model

  With Yjs, the live document is no longer the thing you save ‚ÄĒ it's a thing you
  snapshot. The authoritative live state lives in a Yjs document (a Y.Doc) held
  by a sync server. Postgres stops being the source of truth during editing and
  becomes a durable snapshot + history store. The whole trick is defining the
  boundary cleanly: who owns truth, and when does it cross into Postgres.

  There are three layers, and you persist at two of them:

  Layer 1 ‚ÄĒ The Yjs update log (the safety net)

  Every keystroke produces a tiny binary Yjs update. The sync server appends
  these to a log ‚ÄĒ either in Postgres (a yjs_updates table: doc_id, seq, 
  update_bytes) or Redis. This is append-only, so there's no overwrite, no race
  ‚ÄĒ it's just "operation N happened." If the server crashes, you replay the log
  to rebuild the exact document. This log is what makes Yjs durable; it's not
  optional.

  Periodically you compact: merge the accumulated updates into a single Yjs
  state vector + document blob, store that, and truncate the log. That's a
  snapshot of the Yjs binary state, not your markdown.

  Layer 2 ‚ÄĒ The materialized script_content + version history (your existing 
  model)

  Here's the seam I warned about. Your app ‚ÄĒ the rundown panel, the markdown
  export, the version-history UI, the search, the publish pipeline ‚ÄĒ all expect
  rundown_items.script_content to be a markdown string. Yjs doesn't speak
  markdown; it holds a ProseMirror document structure. So you need a
  materialization step:

  ‚Ėé Yjs doc ‚Üí ProseMirror doc ‚Üí docToMarkdown() ‚Üí script_content in Postgres.

  This is the only place a last-write-wins clobber can still happen, and the
  rule that prevents it is: serialization is single-writer and 
  server-authoritative. You do not let each client serialize-and-save its own
  copy (that's literally today's bug). Instead:

  - The sync server (the one process that holds the canonical Y.Doc) is the only
  writer of script_content.
  - It materializes on a debounce ‚ÄĒ e.g. 2 seconds after edits go quiet, or
  every N seconds during sustained editing ‚ÄĒ from the merged Yjs state, never
  from a client's view.
  - Because there's exactly one writer materializing from one merged state, two
  users can't overwrite each other at the DB layer. The merge already happened
  upstream in the CRDT.

  That single-writer rule is the whole answer to "how do you save safely." The
  409 conflict check I designed for the interim fix is the degenerate version of
  this ‚ÄĒ and if you ever have multiple sync-server instances, you still want it
  as a backstop on the script_content write.

  Layer 3 ‚ÄĒ Version snapshots

  Your content_versions table stays almost exactly as-is, but the trigger
  changes: instead of "every autosave," you snapshot on meaningful boundaries ‚ÄĒ
  a debounced quiet period, an explicit "save version" action, or every N
  minutes. The server writes one version row from the merged state, with
  created_by becoming "whoever touched it since the last snapshot" (or a list).
  No more per-keystroke version spam from two thrashing clients.

  The shape of the actual save flow

  Putting it together, here's what "saving" becomes:

  1. Client types ‚Üí Yjs update generated locally ‚Üí broadcast to sync server +
  peers (instant, optimistic, conflict-free merge).
  2. Sync server appends the update to the durable log (Layer¬†1) ‚ÄĒ the real
  "save," and it can't clobber.
  3. On a debounce, the sync server materializes the merged doc ‚Üí markdown ‚Üí
  writes script_content (Layer¬†2), as the sole writer.
  4. On boundaries, the sync server writes a content_versions row (Layer¬†3).

  Clients never PUT script_content anymore. The save-rundown write path for
  script content gets retired, not patched ‚ÄĒ which, notably, also retires the
  whole class of vestigial guards from #33, because the condition they protect
  against (a client overwriting the DB with a stale blob) no longer exists in
  the architecture.

  The two hard parts to budget for

  1. Markdown ‚ÜĒ Yjs fidelity. Your markdownToDoc/docToMarkdown round-trip (and
  your cue nodes, speaker attrs, etc.) must be lossless under Yjs, because
  materialization runs constantly and any lossy serialization becomes permanent
  corruption. You already have assertNoLoss guards ‚ÄĒ those become critical
  infrastructure here.
  2. Initial load / reconciliation. When a doc opens and there's both a Yjs
  snapshot and a script_content in Postgres, which wins? Rule: Yjs state is 
  authoritative if it exists; script_content is only the seed used to build the
  first Y.Doc when no Yjs state exists yet. Getting this ordering wrong is how
  you "lose" content on first migration of each segment.

  The honest summary

  You persist in two places for two reasons: the Yjs update log for durability
  and crash-recovery (append-only, can't clobber), and a debounced server-side 
  materialization to script_content + content_versions for everything else in
  your app that needs markdown. The safety property is one sentence: exactly one
  writer (the sync server) materializes from the merged CRDT state ‚ÄĒ clients 
  never save the document blob. That's the discipline that makes the persistence
  boundary safe; everything else is plumbing.

  Want me to write this up as a proper architecture doc / plan file (it's
  substantial enough to deserve one), or sketch what the sync-server service and
  the yjs_updates/materialization schema would concretely look like in your
  stack?

‚ĚĮ Why do you keep talking in terms of multi week? Because I don't know how you  
  get your time frames, but your time frames are always way off. You've said    
  that multi week projects will take multiple weeks and then end up completing  
  them in thirty minutes.                                                       

‚óŹ You're right, and that's a fair callout. My "multi-week" was lazy ‚ÄĒ I was
  reaching for a generic software-industry estimate instead of reasoning about
  what the work actually is in this codebase, done by an agent that doesn't get 
  tired, context-switch, or wait on standups.

  Let me own the specific error: human-team estimates assume meetings, ramp-up,
  code review latency, people working in parallel and blocking each other, 6
  productive hours a day. None of that applies to how we actually work here.
  When I say "weeks," I'm importing a calendar that doesn't exist in this
  session. You've watched me knock out things I'd have called "multi-day" in a
  single pass ‚ÄĒ because the real constraint is number and difficulty of edits + 
  verification loops, not calendar time.

  So let me re-estimate the Yjs persistence work the honest way ‚ÄĒ in terms of
  the actual concrete units of work, which is the only thing I can reason about:

  - Sync server (y-websocket + persistence) ‚ÄĒ a known, well-trodden setup.
  Mostly wiring an existing library, not inventing. A handful of files.
  - yjs_updates table + append/compact + materialization-to-script_content ‚ÄĒ
  schema migration + one service. Bounded.
  - Single-writer debounced materialization ‚ÄĒ the conceptually careful part, but
  it's one well-defined function with a clear correctness rule.
  - markdownToDoc/docToMarkdown fidelity under Yjs ‚ÄĒ this is the one genuinely
  open-risk item, because I can't know how clean the round-trip is until I
  stress it. That's not "weeks," it's "a verification loop whose length depends
  on what breaks."
  - Initial load / reconciliation rule ‚ÄĒ one ordering decision, small code.

  The truthful framing isn't "weeks." It's: most of this is bounded, known work 
  I can do in a focused sitting or two; one part (markdown‚ÜĒYjs round-trip

‚óŹ This is genuinely good news for the estimate, and it sharpens the real risk.
  Let me note what I'm seeing, then check the backend/deploy side (the other
  half of the work) before giving you the number.

  Key finding: The schema is small and clean ‚ÄĒ only two real node types
  (paragraph with a few attrs, cue as an atom with a fields map). And
  critically, the cue.fields attribute is a plain object (fields: { default: {} 
  }). That matters a lot: a ProseMirror atom whose entire payload is a JS-object
  attribute is a known Yjs gotcha ‚ÄĒ y-prosemirror syncs node attributes as
  opaque values, so two people editing the same cue's fields concurrently is
  last-write-wins on that attribute, not character-merged. Paragraph text merges
  beautifully; cue-internal field edits don't. That's not a blocker ‚ÄĒ it's a
  scope decision (cues are edited via modals anyway, so attribute-level LWW on a
  cue is probably acceptable) ‚ÄĒ but it's exactly the kind of thing that turns
  an estimate from a guess into a real number.

  Let me check the deployment/backend surface ‚ÄĒ that's the other half.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ ICR check-in: nothing actionable ‚ÄĒ just acknowledgments of the push from
  1ed2f6 and 75a12d. Good, the push is verified by others.

  Now, your question. Yes, a separate worktree is the right call ‚ÄĒ and it's 
  clearly better than a branch-in-place here. Let me explain why, then propose
  the concrete setup.

  Why a worktree, not just a branch

  The deciding factor is active multi-session work on this repo. Right now:
  - You (this session) are in /home/kevin/show-build-migration on
  feat/script-editor-tiptap.
  - Session 1ed2f6 is actively committing to that same branch in this same 
  folder (autoscrub work).
  - The Yjs project is exploratory and disruptive ‚ÄĒ it rips out the save path,
  adds a sync server, and stress-tests the editor's core. That's exactly the
  kind of work that shouldn't share a working directory with people shipping
  incremental fixes.

  A plain branch in this folder would mean git checkout toggling everyone's
  files out from under each other. A worktree gives the Yjs work its own 
  directory on its own branch, while feat/script-editor-tiptap keeps running
  untouched here. Both check out of the same repo, no collisions.

  There's a wrinkle worth flagging from CLAUDE.md, though:

  The dev-environment constraint

  This repo is special ‚ÄĒ /home/kevin/show-build-migration isn't just code, it's
  what dev.showbuild.app actually runs: the dev backend container bind-mounts
  /home/kevin/show-build-migration/app, and the dev frontend serves from here. A
  worktree gets you isolated source, but it does not automatically get you a
  running dev environment to test Yjs against ‚ÄĒ and Yjs fundamentally needs a
  running backend (the sync server) + frontend to test at all.

  So the honest picture is: a worktree cleanly isolates the code, but standing
  up a testable Yjs dev stack (sync-server container, pointing a frontend at it)
  is its own small setup task. That's fine ‚ÄĒ it's part of Phase 1 ‚ÄĒ but I don't
  want to imply "make a worktree and you're ready to build." You're ready to
  write code in isolation; running it needs the stack.

  Let me get your intent before I create anything:

‚ĚĮ what is phase4 spike?                                                         

‚óŹ Good question ‚ÄĒ I threw that term around without defining it. Let me unpack it
  plainly.

  First, "spike" as a term

  A spike is a short, throwaway experiment whose only output is an answer, not
  shippable code. You build the riskiest 10% of a project first, in a branch you
  fully intend to delete, purely to find out "is this going to be a problem or
  not?" You're buying information, not features. The code gets thrown away; the
  knowledge is what you keep.

  Why "Phase 4" specifically

  When I broke the Yjs project into phases earlier, four of the five were things
  I could estimate confidently because they're known, library-wired work:
  - Phase 1 ‚ÄĒ stand up the sync server (known library)
  - Phase 2 ‚ÄĒ add the collaboration extension to the editor (known)
  - Phase 3 ‚ÄĒ the save/persistence-to-Postgres logic (careful but well-defined)
  - Phase 5 ‚ÄĒ live cursors/presence (known)

  Phase 4 was the one I tagged "unknown ‚ÄĒ low confidence." That's the part I
  can't estimate from reading the code, and it's the reason my whole timeline
  has a fuzzy range instead of a number.

  What Phase 4 actually is

  Phase 4 is: does your editor's custom machinery still work once Yjs owns the 
  document?

  Here's the tension. Right now ScriptEditor owns its ProseMirror document, and
  you've got a stack of custom plugins built on top of it ‚ÄĒ BlockMultiSelect
  (Ctrl/Shift selecting blocks), BlockDragHandle (drag-reorder), LineNumbers,
  CollapseSummary, the cue NodeView, and the rawMarkdownContent serialization
  bridge. All of those assume they control the editor's transactions.

  When you add Yjs, Yjs takes over as the document owner. Every edit now flows
  through Yjs's sync layer, and Yjs has its own ideas about transactions, undo
  history, and how the document mutates. The open question is whether your
  custom plugins cooperate or collide with that:

  - Does multi-select-delete still apply as one clean operation, or does Yjs
  fragment it?
  - Does drag-reorder of a block sync correctly to other users, or corrupt?
  - Do the cue atoms ‚ÄĒ with that fields object-attribute I flagged ‚ÄĒ survive
  concurrent editing, or does one user's cue edit clobber another's?
  - Does your markdown serialization still round-trip losslessly when Yjs is in
  the middle?

  I genuinely cannot know the answer by reading the code. It depends on runtime
  behavior ‚ÄĒ how two real libraries interact under concurrent edits. It could be
  "they just work, additive, done in an afternoon," or it could be "the drag
  plugin fights Yjs's transaction model and needs reworking," which is the thing
  that would dominate the whole estimate.

  So the Phase-4 spike is

  A few-hour throwaway: make a scratch branch, wire the Yjs Collaboration
  extension into ScriptEditor, open two browser windows, and just try it ‚ÄĒ type
  in both, multi-select, drag a block, edit a cue. Watch what breaks.

  The output isn't code you keep. The output is: "Phase 4 is trivial" or "Phase 
  4 is the hard part, and here's specifically what fights." That single result
  collapses ~80% of the uncertainty in the timeline, because it's the only phase
  I'm unsure about.

  That's why I keep offering it: you asked for a real estimate, and the most
  honest way to give you one is to spend a few hours killing the one unknown
  rather than pad the whole thing with a guess. It's the cheapest possible way
  to turn "somewhere between a couple days and much longer" into an actual
  number.

  Does that clear it up? And if so ‚ÄĒ do you want me to run that spike (throwaway
  worktree, doesn't touch any of your committed work), or would you rather talk
  through the workspace setup first?

‚ĚĮ Okay. Instead of that, can we make it so that only one user can edit a document at a time and another user gets locked out if the first user is already editing it? And then the second user gets the ability to kick out the first user. 

‚óŹ That's a meaningful pivot, and honestly a much more pragmatic one. Let me make
  sure I capture it correctly and reconcile it with everything we just
  discussed.

  What you're now describing

  Single-writer locking instead of real-time collaboration. One user holds an
  editing lock on a script; a second user who opens it gets a read-only / 
  locked-out view, but with an override ‚ÄĒ they can kick out the first user and
  take the lock themselves.

  Why this is a smart change

  This is the "Layer E" option I flagged earlier as a possible direction for #33
  ‚ÄĒ and it has big advantages over the Yjs route:

  - It actually fixes the data-loss bug directly. If only one person can write
  at a time, the concurrent-edit clobber that hit 0276 simply can't happen. No
  CRDT, no sync server, no persistence-boundary subtlety.
  - It's dramatically smaller ‚ÄĒ days of bounded work, not the Yjs unknown. And
  critically, the infrastructure already exists: I found SegmentLock in the
  models and active-edit/register calls already firing in selectRundownItem. The
  locking machinery is already there ‚ÄĒ it's just advisory/UI-only and not 
  enforced on the save path. Your feature is largely "make the existing lock
  real + add the UI."
  - No worktree gymnastics, no spike needed ‚ÄĒ the Phase-4 unknown that was
  making Yjs fuzzy evaporates entirely.

  How this reshapes the todos

  - #40 (Yjs collaboration) ‚Üí I'd downgrade/park this, not delete it. It's still
  the eventual "true Google Docs" end-state if you ever want it, but it's no
  longer the plan. The locking approach is the new direction.
  - This new locking feature largely is the #33 fix ‚ÄĒ single-writer locking is
  the cleanest possible answer to "two people clobbered each other." So I'd fold
  it together with #33 rather than make it a third parallel thing.

  Let me verify the existing lock infrastructure so the new todo is grounded in
  what's actually there, then I'll file it correctly.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Let me look at the SegmentLock model and the existing lock service to
  understand exactly what's there vs. what's needed.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ This is a great finding for grounding the new approach. The picture is now
  crystal clear:

  What already exists:
  - A full SegmentLock DB model ‚ÄĒ rundown_item_asset_id, user_id, locked_at,
  expires_at, last_heartbeat. It's purpose-built for exactly this feature
  ("Segment-level editing locks for concurrent editing protection"). It even has
  heartbeat + expiry columns for stale-lock cleanup.
  - register_active_edit/unregister_active_edit in housekeeping (but that's a
  separate, weaker in-memory ACTIVE_EDITS dict, not the DB lock).
  - Lock enforcement on episode delete, and a frontend lock dialog in
  EpisodesView.

  What's missing (= your feature):
  - The SegmentLock is not acquired when you open a segment to edit, and not 
  enforced on the save path. So it exists but does nothing for the edit
  workflow.
  - No "you're locked out, this is read-only" UI on the editor.
  - No "take over / kick out" override.

  So your feature is mostly wiring up infrastructure that's already half-built ‚ÄĒ
  far smaller than Yjs. This is the right call. Let me reconcile the todos:

‚óŹ Done. Here's how I reconciled the pivot across the whole concurrency thread.

  The decision and why it's a good one

  You've swapped real-time collaboration for single-writer locking with 
  take-over ‚ÄĒ and that's the more pragmatic call. The big finding: the 
  infrastructure is already half-built. There's a SegmentLock DB model
  (app/models/content.py:11) purpose-built for exactly this ‚ÄĒ user_id,
  expires_at, last_heartbeat, the works ‚ÄĒ and it's already enforced on episode 
  delete with a lock dialog already in the UI. It just isn't wired into the
  editing workflow. So your feature is mostly activating existing 
  infrastructure, not building from scratch ‚ÄĒ far smaller than Yjs, and it fixes
  #33 directly (no two writers = no clobber).

  Todo list, now reconciled

  - #41 (new, normal) ‚ÄĒ Single-writer segment editing lock + take-over/kick-out.
  The chosen fix. Plan grounded in the existing SegmentLock infra: what's there
  (model, delete-enforcement, lock dialog) vs. what's missing (acquire-on-open,
  save-path enforcement, read-only UI, take-over endpoint+modal,
  heartbeat/expiry). Notably it reuses the editable prop I added for the version
  preview (81d49d3) for the locked-out read-only editor.
  - #40 (Yjs, ‚Üí on_hold/parked) ‚ÄĒ kept as the long-term "true Google-Docs
  co-editing" end-state, flagged as an ultraplan candidate if ever revived, but
  explicitly not the current plan.
  - #33 (data-loss) ‚ÄĒ updated to point at #41 as the chosen fix; the
  investigation's server-side conflict-check idea is folded into #41 as the
  save-path backstop.

  Plan files written (no repo code touched)

  - todo-41-segment-locking.md ‚ÄĒ full design for the locking feature
  - todo-40-yjs-collab-PLAN.md ‚ÄĒ the parked Yjs plan, preserved with the Phase-4
  spike note
  - README-INDEX.md ‚ÄĒ updated with the #33 ‚Üí #41 ‚Üí #40 concurrency thread

  One design call worth surfacing now, since it's the only non-trivial part of
  #41: how the kicked-out user finds out they've been evicted. I recommend
  starting simple ‚ÄĒ the editor already heartbeats to keep its lock alive, so the
  heartbeat response can just say "you no longer hold this lock" and flip it to
  read-only. That avoids needing new SSE/WebSocket plumbing. Good enough, or do
  you want eviction to feel instant (which would mean wiring the real-time
  channel)?

‚óŹ Eviction copy is captured. Now you've said implement the feature ‚ÄĒ #41, the
  single-writer segment lock with take-over.

  This is a substantial full-stack feature (DB lock acquire/enforce, save-path
  enforcement, heartbeat, take-over endpoint, read-only UI, eviction notice).
  Before I write code, two quick coordination points that genuinely affect how I
  build it ‚ÄĒ I'll lay them out and proceed with sensible defaults rather than
  block you, since you said go.

  Decisions I'm making (tell me if any are wrong):
  1. Eviction signal = heartbeat-poll, not new WebSocket plumbing. The editor
  already needs a heartbeat to keep its lock alive; the heartbeat response tells
  an evicted user they've lost the lock. Simplest, no new infra. (Latency ‚Čą one
  heartbeat interval, ~15-30s ‚ÄĒ acceptable for this.)
  2. Lock granularity = per rundown item (matches
  SegmentLock.rundown_item_asset_id).
  3. Build on this branch (feat/script-editor-tiptap), committing per-layer,
  coordinating with 1ed2f6 on the relay since it touches ContentEditor.vue.

  script content is being written. Let me add the check right where 'script' in 
  item_data is detected.

  is the genuinely new piece. It evicts the current holder and returns who was
