‚ĚĮ sometimes, when i leave a segment as kevin, that segment may still be grey    
  out on joshs account and show as locked.                                      

‚óŹ That's the stale-lock symptom ‚ÄĒ kevin's release isn't always propagating, so
  josh keeps seeing the lock. There are a few possible causes; let me find the
  actual one rather than guess. The key question: when kevin leaves a segment,
  does releaseLock reliably fire AND get cleaned from the DB, and does josh's 4s
  poll then drop it?

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ No locks in the DB right now (clean). The release at line 4714 is guarded by
  this.segmentLockState.currentAssetId.value === prevAssetId. That guard is the
  likely culprit: if currentAssetId doesn't match prevAssetId (e.g., a race
  where it was already cleared, or acquire was still in-flight and hadn't set
  currentAssetId yet), the release is skipped ‚ÄĒ leaving the lock in the DB until
  its TTL expires.

  Let me read that block and the releaseLock composable to confirm the failure
  modes.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Found multiple failure modes:

  1. The currentAssetId === prevAssetId guard (line¬†4714): releaseLock() reads
  currentAssetId.value to know what to release. If acquire on the new item
  already ran and overwrote currentAssetId, OR if there was a race, the guard
  fails and release is skipped.
  2. releaseLock() releases currentAssetId.value, not prevAssetId ‚ÄĒ let me check
  the composable. If by the time release runs currentAssetId has changed, it
  releases the wrong (or no) lock.
  3. Bigger issue: this release only fires on item switch within the same
  session. If kevin closes the tab, navigates away, or the component unmounts,
  the lock is NOT released (I saw earlier onUnmounted deliberately doesn't
  release). It then lingers until TTL.

  Let me check the composable's releaseLock to confirm what it actually
  releases.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Confirmed: releaseLock() releases currentAssetId.value. So the chain is
  fragile in two ways:
  - The guard in ContentEditor (currentAssetId === prevAssetId) can skip the
  release.
  - Even when it runs, it releases whatever currentAssetId happens to be at that
  moment ‚ÄĒ which could have been changed by a concurrent acquire.

  The robust fix: release prevAssetId explicitly, not via the stateful
  currentAssetId. Let me make releaseLock accept an optional explicit asset id,
  and call it unconditionally with prevAssetId (drop the fragile guard). Also
  add a release-by-id so it doesn't depend on composable state.

  First, update the composable to accept an explicit asset id:

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Now the bigger gap: tab close / navigate away / component unmount doesn't

‚óŹ There IS a releaseLock() on unmount (line 1022) ‚ÄĒ good. But the real gap is
  tab close / browser refresh, where Vue's beforeUnmount may not fire reliably
  or the async axios call gets killed mid-flight. The robust fix for that is a
  releaseAllLocks via navigator.sendBeacon on pagehide/beforeunload (sendBeacon
  survives page unload, unlike axios).

  But before adding more, let me reconsider ‚ÄĒ the simplest, most reliable safety
  net already exists: the lock has a TTL (LOCK_TTL_SECONDS = 60), and the
  backend's cleanup_expired_locks runs on every lock query (including josh's
  /active poll). So a truly abandoned lock auto-clears within 60s. The problem
  you're seeing is likely the race-skipped release (now fixed) making locks
  linger the full 60s instead of clearing instantly.

  Let me check what the TTL actually is ‚ÄĒ if it's 60s but the heartbeat extends
  it, an abandoned lock could persist 60s after last heartbeat. Let me verify
  the heartbeat interval vs TTL.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Important: /active (line 62 area) calls cleanup_expired_locks, so josh's poll
  auto-clears expired locks. But the TTL is 60s and heartbeat extends by 60s
  every 15s. So an abandoned lock (kevin closed tab) lingers up to 60s ‚ÄĒ that's
  a long time to see a stale grey row.

  The combination of fixes for your symptom:
  1. The race-skipped release (now fixed ‚ÄĒ explicit prevAssetId) ‚Üí handles the
  normal "kevin switched segments" case instantly.
  2. TTL is too long for abandonment ‚Üí shorten it so a closed-tab lock clears
  faster. 60s heartbeat-extension with 15s heartbeat is excessive; let me
  tighten TTL to ~30s so an abandoned lock clears within 30s.

  Let me reduce the TTL/extension to 30s (still 2√ó the 15s heartbeat, so live
  editors never lapse).

‚óŹ Now let me also add a pagehide beacon release for the tab-close case ‚ÄĒ
  navigator.sendBeacon survives unload. But the release endpoint is a POST that
  needs auth; sendBeacon can't set headers. Given the TTL now handles

  adding sendBeacon (which needs a token-in-body endpoint) is extra complexity.
  Let me keep it simpler: the 30s TTL + explicit release covers it.

‚óŹ Done ‚ÄĒ committed (7a9491d), backend healthy and restarted.

  Two causes, both fixed

  1. The release was getting skipped in a race (the main culprit). When kevin
  left a segment, the release was guarded by currentAssetId === prevAssetId and
  released whatever currentAssetId happened to be at that moment. If the acquire
  for the new segment had already overwritten currentAssetId (a timing race,
  made more likely by the fire-and-forget change), the guard failed and the 
  release never fired ‚ÄĒ leaving kevin's lock on the old segment in the DB,
  greying it on josh's screen.

  Fix: releaseLock(assetId) now takes an explicit target, and ContentEditor
  releases prevAssetId unconditionally on every switch. No more race-skipped
  releases.

  2. Abandoned locks lingered too long. If kevin closed the tab or navigated
  away without a clean release, the lock sat until its 60-second TTL expired.
  Shortened the TTL (and heartbeat-extension) to 30 seconds ‚ÄĒ still double the
  15s client heartbeat, so a live editor's lock never lapses, but an abandoned
  one now clears within ~30s. The backend's cleanup_expired_locks runs on josh's
  /active poll, so his row un-greys automatically.

  To test

  Have kevin switch between segments a bunch ‚ÄĒ josh's grey rows should now clear
  within his ~4s poll, not stay stuck. And if kevin just closes his tab, josh's
  stale row should clear within ~30s.

  That should resolve the stuck-grey behavior. The release-race fix is the
  important one; the TTL change is the backstop for the tab-close case.

  The unpushed #41 stack keeps growing (6b9a713..7a9491d). Want me to push the
  batch now (coordinating with 1ed2f6)? It'd be a good idea to get this safely
  on origin.

  - Pushed: 15 commits (6b9a713..7a9491d) to origin/feat/script-editor-tiptap,

  What shipped in #41

  Single-writer segment locking with: lock acquire/release, take-over + eviction
  ("You have been evicted‚Ä¶"), the read-only Segment Locked overlay (centered on
  viewport, Cancel + ESC dismiss), rundown grey-out + framed red padlock + name
  badge on locked rows, ~4s lock-sync polling, and the release-race + 30s-TTL
  fixes. Plus rundown polish that came along the way (right-aligned durations,
  char-count-under-duration, revisions moved to the ‚čģ menu, region-duration
  alignment).

  Known rough edge (documented, not blocking): occasional lock-sync timing ‚ÄĒ
  locks propagate in ~4s via polling, and a true sub-second push would need an
  async server stack (the SSE attempt starved the sync gunicorn workers, so we
  reverted to polling).

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ The issue is likely listener order: App.vue registers handleGlobalHotkey (and
  installModalStackHandler) in setup() ‚ÄĒ which runs before EditorPanel's
  onMounted registers handleGlobalKeydown. Document keydown listeners fire in
  registration order, so App.vue's handlers run first. If the modal-stack
  handler or handleGlobalHotkey does preventDefault/stopPropagation on ESC,
  EditorPanel never sees it.

  But more importantly ‚ÄĒ the EditorPanel ESC handler I wrote uses
  event.stopPropagation(), which only stops later listeners, not earlier ones.
  So if App.vue consumes ESC first, EditorPanel's handler is bypassed.

  The robust fix: register the lock-overlay ESC handler in the capture phase so
  it runs before App.vue's bubble-phase handlers. Let me add a dedicated
  capture-phase ESC listener that's active only while the overlay shows.
  Actually simplest: add { capture: true } to a small dedicated listener for the
  overlay ESC. Let me add a focused capture listener.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ I'll add a dedicated capture-phase ESC handler for the lock overlay, so it
  intercepts ESC before App.vue's bubble-phase handlers can consume it. Let me
  add the listener and a handler function.

‚óŹ Done ‚ÄĒ committed (7360a2c) and live on dev. Two things:

  1. Editable segment title

  The title at the top of the script editor (the "Enter a segment title" /
  segment name) was a static span ‚ÄĒ now it's a borderless inline input. Click it
  and type; it persists through updateMetadata('title', ...) ‚ÄĒ the same path
  the metadata-panel Title field uses, so it stays in sync. Enter commits/blurs.
  The placeholder and styling match the old look exactly.

  2. #41 follow-up ‚ÄĒ ESC on the Segment Locked overlay

  You were right that ESC wasn't backing you out. I had added an ESC handler in
  b87bc00, but it was a bubble-phase handler ‚ÄĒ and App.vue registers its
  global/modal-stack ESC handlers first (in setup(), before EditorPanel mounts),
  so App.vue was consuming ESC before EditorPanel ever saw it.

  Fix: I added a dedicated capture-phase ESC listener for the lock overlay.
  Capture phase runs before bubble phase, so it now intercepts ESC first and
  dismisses the overlay reliably, regardless of what other handlers are
  registered. (It only acts when the overlay is actually showing, so it doesn't
  interfere with anything else.)

  So now you can press ESC to back out of the "Segment Locked" modal ‚ÄĒ plus the
  Cancel button and backdrop-click still work.

  Reload to test both.

  ICR check-in ‚ÄĒ done, nothing actionable.

  These are 1 commit, not yet pushed. Want me to keep going on the title/editor,
  or anything else?

‚óŹ That's a thorough map. Let me answer your question directly, then figure out
  what your new query needs.

  Is it all one pipeline? Mostly yes ‚ÄĒ with a clear canonical path

  There is one unified config + call layer, but there are a couple of variants
  by use case:

  - The provider/config brain is centralized: every call resolves
  model/host/temperature/prompt from the api_configs table (per-workflow
  overrides ‚Üí global Ollama ‚Üí hardcoded qwen3:32b default), via
  ollama_resolver.py. Prompts live in the prompt_overrides table (or code
  defaults).
  - The canonical call function for background generation is call_ollama() in
  auto_description_service.py (wraps ollama_resolver.call_with_fallback, which
  auto-retries a fallback model on¬†404).
  - Three flavors of the same path: (1) background/Celery on the llm_content
  queue (auto-description, tone, episode description) ‚ÄĒ this is the dominant
  pattern; (2) sync request-level (SegmentLLMExtractor for structured
  extraction); (3) frontend proxy (/api/llm/ollama/generate) for
  browser-triggered calls.
  - The canonical pattern for a generated field is the history pattern:
  generate_{field} / regenerate_{field} + a {field}_gen_history JSONB column +
  the llm_generated_fields array (which drives the purple-highlight UI).

  So: one config brain, one call layer, with async-vs-sync-vs-proxy depending on
  trigger. A new query should hook into call_ollama() + api_configs rather than
  spinning up its own HTTP client.

  Now ‚ÄĒ what do you want to build?

  The right shape depends heavily on the specifics, and a few answers change the
  design materially. Let me get them:

  Design: LLM slug generation for segments

  The rule (your spec, captured):
  - Input: title + script_content (body, if any).
  - Output: a broadcast slug, 2‚Äď4 words (prefer 2‚Äď3), lowercase,
  hyphen/space-joined per existing slug rules.
  - Triggers:
    a. Auto (background): slug is empty ‚Üí generate from title+body.
    b. Auto (shorten): existing slug is ‚Č•5 words ‚Üí regenerate; the prompt
  includes the long slug + title + body and asks for a shorter broadcast
  version.
    c. Manual: a generate/regenerate button on the slug field (per "both").

      245      """Eligibility for the auto sweep: empty slug OR a too-lon
           g (>=5 word) one."""
      246      wc = _word_count(slug or "")
      247      return wc == 0 or wc >= LONG_SLUG_TRIGGER_WORDS
      248 +                                                              
      249 +                                                              
      250 +@shared_task(name="services.slug_gen_service.sweep_segments_fo
          +r_slug_generation")                                           
      251 +def sweep_segments_for_slug_generation():                     
      252 +    """Periodic task (Beat). Finds one segment whose slug need
          +s (re)generation ‚ÄĒ                                            
      253 +    empty OR >= 5 words ‚ÄĒ with real content, and generates a s
          +hort slug. Never                                              
      254 +    overwrites a user's valid 1-4 word slug. Word-count is com
          +puted in SQL                                                  
      255 +    (separators are space/hyphen/underscore) so the query only
          + returns the two                                              
      256 +    eligible cases.                                           
      257 +    """                                                       
      258 +    # Health-gate the LLM like the description sweep does.    
      259 +    try:                                                      
      260 +        host = get_ollama_host(SessionLocal())                
      261 +        import requests                                       
      262 +        h = requests.get(f"{host}/api/tags", timeout=5)       
      263 +        if h.status_code != 200:                              
      264 +            return {"status": "skipped", "reason": "ollama_unh
          +ealthy"}                                                      
      265 +    except Exception:                                         
      266 +        return {"status": "skipped", "reason": "ollama_unreach
          +able"}                                                        
      267 +                                                              
      268 +    db = SessionLocal()                                       
      269 +    try:                                                      
      270 +        # regexp_count of word separators + 1 gives the word c
          +ount; eligible when                                           
      271 +        # slug is blank OR has >= LONG_SLUG_TRIGGER_WORDS word
          +s.                                                            
      272 +        row = db.execute(text("""                             
      273 +            SELECT ri.asset_id                                
      274 +            FROM rundown_items ri                             
      275 +            JOIN rundowns r ON r.id = ri.rundown_id           
      276 +            JOIN episodes e ON e.id = r.episode_id            
      277 +            WHERE e.status IN ('draft', 'production')         
      278 +              AND COALESCE(e.auto_generate_enabled, TRUE) = TR
          +UE                                                            
      279 +              AND ri.item_type IN ('segment', 'interview', 'co
          +ldopen', 'close')                                             
      280 +              AND LENGTH(COALESCE(ri.script_content, '')) >= 1
          +00                                                            
      281 +              AND ri.updated_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '2 minutes'
      282 +              AND (                                           
      283 +                    LENGTH(TRIM(COALESCE(ri.slug, ''))) = 0   
      284 +                    OR (regexp_count(TRIM(ri.slug), '[\\s_-]+'
          +) + 1) >= :longwords                                          
      285 +                  )                                           
      286 +            ORDER BY e.air_date ASC NULLS LAST, ri.order_in_ru
          +ndown ASC                                                     
      287 +            LIMIT 1                                           
      288 +        """), {"longwords": LONG_SLUG_TRIGGER_WORDS}).fetchone
          +()                                                            
      289 +                                                              
      290 +        if not row:                                           
      291 +            return {"status": "idle"}                         
      292 +                                                              
      293 +        asset_id = row.asset_id                               
      294 +        slug = _generate_slug_core(db, asset_id)              
      295 +        logger.info(f"[slug-sweep] Generated slug for {asset_i
          +d}: {slug}")                                                  
      296 +        return {"status": "processed", "asset_id": asset_id, "
          +slug": slug}                                                  
      297 +    except Exception as e:                                    
      298 +        logger.error(f"[slug-sweep] Error: {e}")              
      299 +        return {"status": "error", "error": str(e)}           
      300 +    finally:                                                  
      301 +        db.close()

‚óŹ Backend clean ‚ÄĒ both /api/episodes/generate-slug and /regenerate-slug are
  registered, no errors. Let me do a live end-to-end test of slug generation

‚óŹ The endpoint works (reached the model) but got an empty slug. Likely the model
  returned something _slugify stripped to empty, OR the Ollama call returned
  empty (model not loaded / the qwen3 model emits <think> reasoning that my
  "first line" extraction discards). qwen3 is a thinking model ‚ÄĒ it outputs
  <think>...</think> then the answer. My _slugify takes the first line, which
  would be the think tag. Let me check the raw output.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ The raw output is empty ‚ÄĒ and with only max_tokens=40, the qwen3 thinking
  model spends all 40 tokens inside <think> and never emits the answer. That's
  the bug: qwen3 is a reasoning model that needs either /no_think or enough
  tokens to finish thinking and answer.

  The fix: (1) append /no_think to the prompt (qwen3 supports this to skip
  reasoning), and (2) strip any <think>...</think> block in _slugify as a safety
  net, and (3) raise max_tokens a bit. Let me update the service.

‚óŹ Update(app/services/slug_gen_service.py)

         + re.IGNORECASE)                                                
      70 +    s = re.sub(r"</?think>", " ", s, flags=re.IGNORECASE)

      72 +    if not s:

      74 +    # Take the last non-empty line (the answer usually follows

      75 +    lines = [ln for ln in s.splitlines() if ln.strip()]

‚óŹ Now bump max_tokens in both calls (generate + regenerate) from 40 to 120 ‚ÄĒ
  enough for the answer even if some thinking leaks. Let me update both.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Now restart and re-test the slug generation.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Still empty. Let me see the raw output again with the /no_think + 120 tokens
  to understand what the model is actually returning.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Still completely empty (len: 0). The model returns nothing ‚ÄĒ this isn't a
  parsing issue, it's the call itself returning empty. Let me test the raw
  Ollama call directly to isolate whether it's the model, the endpoint, or the
  /no_think confusing it.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Found it. done_reason: length, eval_count: 50 ‚ÄĒ the model used all 50 tokens
  but produced an empty response because qwen3 spent all tokens inside <think>
  reasoning that Ollama strips/buffers, and num_predict cut it off before the
  answer. This is the core qwen3 behavior: it needs either many more tokens OR
  proper /no_think handling. My prompt-appended /no_think didn't take effect
  (qwen3 needs it differently, or the model build ignores it).

  The reliable fix: raise num_predict substantially so the model finishes
  thinking AND answers. A slug needs few output tokens but qwen3's thinking can
  be long. Let me test with a high token budget.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ /no_think DOES work ‚ÄĒ with num_predict=500, the response is 'Hello' (clean,
  done=stop, only used ~170 tokens because /no_think skipped reasoning). The
  problem was purely num_predict too low (40-120 wasn't enough headroom even
  with no_think, because the prompt prefix matters). With 500 it works
  perfectly.

  So the fix: bump max_tokens to ~500 (it stops early anyway via /no_think). Let
  me update both slug calls to 500.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

      918 +// LLM slug generation (todo: slug-gen feature). Generates a s
          +hort broadcast                                                
      919 +// slug from the title + script body; if the current slug is 5
          ++ words the                                                   
      920 +// backend includes it and shortens it. The result is emitted 
          +as a field update                                             
      921 +// so it persists through the normal path.                    
      922 +const generatingSlug = ref(false)                             
      923 +async function generateSlug() {                               
      924 +  if (!props.item || !(props.item.asset_id || props.item.id)) 
          +return                                                        
      925 +  const assetId = props.item.asset_id || props.item.id        
      926 +  generatingSlug.value = true                                 
      927 +  try {                                                       
      928 +    const response = await $axios.post(`/episodes/generate-slu
          +g`, { asset_id: assetId })                                    
      929 +    if (response.data && response.data.slug) {                
      930 +      emit('update-segment-field', { field: 'slug', value: res
          +ponse.data.slug })                                            
      931 +    }                                                         
      932 +  } catch (error) {                                           
      933 +    console.error('Slug generation failed:', error)           
      934 +    if (window.notifyUserStandard) {                          
      935 +      const msg = error.response?.data?.detail || 'Slug genera
          +tion failed'                                                  
      936 +      window.notifyUserStandard(msg, '#F44336', 4000)         
      937 +    }                                                         
      938 +  } finally {

‚óŹ Done ‚ÄĒ the segment slug generation feature is built, tested end-to-end on dev,
  and committed (9ce8784).

  What it does

  Generates a short broadcast slug (2‚Äď4 words, prefer 2‚Äď3) from the segment's
  title + script body ‚ÄĒ built squarely on the existing canonical LLM pipeline
  (call_ollama + api_configs config resolution + the gen_history pattern),
  exactly like auto-description.

  Triggers (both, as you asked):
  - Manual: a purple mdi-auto-fix button on the slug field in MetadataPanel.
  - Auto: a 90-second background sweep, eligible only when the slug is empty or
  5+ words (the shorten case) ‚ÄĒ never overwrites a valid 1‚Äď4 word slug.
  - Shortening: when the existing slug is 5+ words, the prompt includes it and
  asks for a shorter broadcast version. (Tested: the-very-long-overly-wordy-slug
  ‚Üí charlie-kirk-shooting.)

  The prompt is exposed and editable in Settings

  Per your request ‚ÄĒ the actual prompt is now in the Settings ‚Üí PromptManager as
  the "Segment Slug Generator" operation. You can View Default (shows the real
  prompt) and override it; the backend reads that override (prompt_overrides,
  category generate, key slug-generator).

  What got built

  - Backend: g021 migration (slug_gen_history column), slug_gen_service.py
  (generate/regenerate/sweep + a _slugify that caps to 4 words and strips qwen3
  <think>), POST /episodes/generate-slug + /regenerate-slug, Celery routing +
  90s beat.
  - Frontend: the slug Generate button + the Settings prompt exposure.

  One real gotcha I hit and fixed

  qwen3 is a thinking model ‚ÄĒ at low token budgets it spent everything inside
  <think> and returned an empty slug. Fixed with max_tokens=500 + /no_think, and
  a <think>-stripping safety net. Worth remembering for future LLM features on
  this stack.

  Status: local commit, not pushed. Worker + beat restarted so the auto-sweep is
  live. Want me to push this (coordinating with 1ed2f6), or test the
  button/auto-sweep in the UI first?

‚ÄĽ recap: We're building Show-Build's segment-editor features; I just finished LLM slug generation (button + auto-sweep + editable prompt in Settings), committed locally but not pushed. Next: push it to origin after coordinating with the other session, or you can test the slug button in the UI first.

‚óŹ Found it. The committed fix is in (8bff149), and here's where the prompt
  lives:

  Where the slug prompt is editable

  Settings ‚Üí "LLM Prompts" tab (SettingsView.vue:58 ‚ÄĒ the tab with the
  mdi-text-box-outline icon). That tab renders the PromptManager component.

  To edit the slug prompt there:
  1. Open Settings ‚Üí LLM Prompts tab.
  2. Add/create a prompt override. Set:
    - Category: generate
    - Operation: "Segment Slug Generator" (operation key slug-generator)
  3. Click "View Default Prompt" to see the actual prompt currently being used
  (the real broadcast-slug prompt with its variables: show_name, segment_title,
  segment_content, has_long_slug, long_slug).
  4. Paste/edit it in the User Prompt Template Override field and save.

  Once you save an enabled override there, the backend's
  get_slug_prompt_template() reads it (from prompt_overrides, category generate,
  key slug-generator) instead of the built-in default ‚ÄĒ so your edits take
  effect on the next generation.

  One honest caveat to flag: I wired the slug operation into PromptManager, but
  I should verify the "View Default Prompt" dialog actually renders cleanly for
  it (the template() function I added takes specific params). If the
  default-viewer errors or the operation doesn't show in the dropdown when you
  try it, tell me what you see and I'll fix the wiring ‚ÄĒ that part I built but
  haven't click-tested in the live UI yet.

  Also done this turn

  The auto-slug sweep now requires the episode to be in production (was
  draft+production) ‚ÄĒ committed 8bff149. Backend + worker restarted, so it's
  live.

  Want to go verify the prompt editor in Settings now, or should I push this
  batch (the slug feature + the production-only fix)?

‚óŹ The v-textarea has a label (Vuetify floating label). The classic symptom: the 
  floating label sits over the first line of text, so the top half of the first
  line looks "faded"/overlapped ‚ÄĒ because the label, when the field has content,
  floats up but overlaps the top of the first text row in some Vuetify
  density/variant configs (especially the default filled variant without enough
  top padding).

  The fix is to use a variant/density that gives the label its own space, or
  move the label out. The cleanest fix matching Vuetify: add variant="outlined"
  (label sits in the border notch, clear of text) ‚ÄĒ or
  persistent-placeholder/density. Let me apply variant="outlined" to the prompt
  textarea (and the notes one for consistency).

  the text ‚ÄĒ so the first line renders fully.

  Prompt Template, Developer Notes) for consistency.

‚óŹ That's the bug. The <v-dialog> teleports its content to <body>, outside the
  .prompt-manager root div. So .prompt-manager :deep(.v-textarea ...) never 
  matches the textarea ‚ÄĒ it's no longer a descendant of .prompt-manager in the
  DOM. That's why my fix (scoped under .prompt-manager) has zero effect on the
  dialog textareas.

  The fix: give the dialog a class and target that (the dialog content can be
  reached because v-dialog's overlay gets the class), OR use a non-scoped global
  override. The cleanest: add a class to the v-dialog (e.g.
  class="prompt-edit-dialog") ‚ÄĒ v-dialog forwards class to the overlay which
  stays in the teleported tree ‚ÄĒ and target the mask under that. Let me add the
  class to the dialog and rewrite the override.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Done ‚ÄĒ committed (1116baa) and live on dev.

  The real root cause (finally)

  The faded top was Vuetify's built-in mask-image gradient on .v-textarea 
  .v-field__input ‚ÄĒ it deliberately fades content near the top so text scrolling
  under the floating label notch disappears gracefully. With your prompt being
  long enough to scroll (you can see the scrollbar in the screenshot), that
  gradient was fading the top of the first line.

  Why my earlier fix didn't work: I'd scoped the override under .prompt-manager,
  but v-dialog teleports its content to <body> ‚ÄĒ completely outside that root
  div. So the selector never matched the textarea, and the mask stayed.

  The fix: I added a prompt-edit-dialog class to the dialog (which rides along
  to the teleported overlay) and override the mask-image: none there. Now the
  selector actually reaches the textarea.

  Reload Settings ‚Üí LLM Prompts ‚Üí edit the slug prompt ‚ÄĒ the first line should
  now be fully opaque, no fade.

  That was a stubborn one (three attempts), but the teleport was the missing
  piece. Let me know if it's finally clean.

  Here's what the current state is, briefly: the script editor uses a custom

  margins (0.32s springy), with a dragged-block ghost, a drop-gap indicator, and
  a post-drop flash. It works on paragraphs + cues (top-level reorder). The map
  flagged these concrete gaps vs. the smoother rundown drag:

  - No auto-scroll when dragging near the top/bottom edge (the scroll-zone
  indicators exist but aren't wired).
  - Multi-select drag isn't implemented ‚ÄĒ dragging a selected block moves only
  that one, not all selected.
  - Margin-based animation (CPU reflow) vs. the rundown's GPU transform/FLIP
  (the "feels rough" issue from todo #39).
  - Drop-indicator recreated each gap-move (re-fires fade-in).

‚ĚĮ when drag is active, the dropline should still be cast as it currently is but the components under it should animate to move out of its way to create the displacement

  and the blocks below it animate downward (transform) to open the displacement

  way, smoothly, rather than the current margin-reflow.

  That actually simplifies and focuses the displacement design: instead of
  pushing the block before up and the block after down (the current two-sided
  margin approach), the cleaner model is ‚ÄĒ everything at/below the drop gap 
  shifts down via translateY to make room, while the indicator sits cast in
  place. Let me update the plan to reflect this precise behavior.

  ‚éŅ ¬†Plan saved to: ~/.claude/plans/hidden-fluttering-allen.md ¬∑ /plan to edit
     Plan ‚ÄĒ Smooth the Script-Editor Drag-and-Drop Animation (todo #39)

     Context

     Reordering blocks in the Script Mode editor feels rough/janky compared to
     the Rundown
     panel, which is smooth. Root cause (verified in code): the script editor's
     custom
     ProseMirror drag plugin animates the "open a gap for the drop" displacement
     with CSS
     margins (margin-top/bottom: 64px, 0.32s springy transition). Margins force
     a full
     layout reflow every frame ‚ÄĒ CPU-bound, hence janky. The Rundown panel uses
     vuedraggable/SortableJS, which animates with GPU-composited transforms + 
     FLIP, so it's
     smooth.

     Goal: make the script drag feel as smooth as the rundown drag. Desired
     live-drag behavior:
     the dropline indicator stays cast in place at the target gap, and the
     blocks below it
     animate out of the way (slide down) to open the displacement space ‚ÄĒ
     smoothly, via GPU
     transforms instead of the current margin reflow. Specifically: (1) drive
     displacement with
     transform: translateY() (blocks at/below the gap shift down) instead of
     margins, (2) stop
     the drop indicator from re-firing its fade on every pointer move
     (reposition, don't
     recreate), and (3) add a real FLIP settle on drop so the moved block glides
     into place
     instead of snapping. Keep the existing open-gap / dashed "DROP HERE" visual
     ‚ÄĒ only the
     motion changes, not the design.

     This is isolated to one file. No backend, no persistence, no schema
     changes.

     Current implementation (all in BlockDragHandle.js)

     - STYLE_TEXT CSS (:66-198): .pm-drag-block has the margin transition
     (:70-76);
     .pm-displace-after = margin-top:64px, .pm-displace-before =
     margin-bottom:64px
     (:130-131); drop-gap widget .pm-drop-gap with pm-drop-gap-in fade
     (:156-172);
     drop flash keyframes (:135-151).
     - nearestGap(view, clientY) (:499-512) ‚ÄĒ computes the target gap from block
     rect midpoints.
     - applyDisplacement(view, gapIndex, sourceIndex) (:518-528) ‚ÄĒ toggles the
     pm-displace-* classes on the persistent block DOM (deliberately not
     decorations, so
     the CSS transition survives).
     - buildMoveTransaction(state, sourceIndex, gapIndex) (:288-315) ‚ÄĒ the
     single-tx move
     (delete + re-insert the same node object; one undo step).
     - flashDrop(view, landedIndex) (:541-556) ‚ÄĒ post-drop pulse on moved block
     + neighbors.
     - The dragged-source glow (.pm-drag-source) and cursor ghost
     (.pm-drag-ghost) already
     use transforms/box-shadow and are fine ‚ÄĒ leave them.

     Approach (recommended)

     1. Displacement: margins ‚Üí transform, "blocks move out of the dropline's 
     way" (the core fix)

     Desired behavior (per Kevin): the dropline/indicator stays cast in place
     where it
     currently appears (at the target gap), and the blocks below it animate
     downward via
     transform: translateY to open the displacement space ‚ÄĒ i.e. the components
     slide out of
     the indicator's way, smoothly, instead of the current two-sided margin
     reflow.

     - Replace the current two-class margin scheme (pm-displace-before push-up +
     pm-displace-after push-down via margins) with a transform-driven downward 
     shift: every
     block at index >= gapIndex (everything at/below the drop point) gets
     transform: translateY(GAP_PX) so the space opens below the cast indicator;
     blocks
     above the gap stay put. The indicator itself does not move once cast ‚ÄĒ the
     rows below it
     glide down to reveal the gap.
     - Animate transform (not margins), tuned to ~`200ms
     cubic-bezier(.2,0,0,1)to match the rundown's:animation="200"` (the current
     0.32s springy overshoot is part of the "rough"
     read). Transforms are GPU-composited ‚Üí no per-frame reflow.
     - applyDisplacement(view, gapIndex, sourceIndex) (:518) changes from
     toggling two classes
     on two specific blocks to toggling a single pm-shift-down class on **all
     blocks at index
     ‚Ėé = gapIndex** (excluding the dragged source). Keep the existing noop guard
     ‚Ėé (gapIndex === source / source+1) so dropping back in place doesn't shift.
     - Add will-change: transform to shifted blocks only while a drag is active
     (toggle a
     pm-dragging-active class on the editor root in startDrag/clear on drop) so
     idle editing
     pays no compositing cost.
     - The .pm-drop-gap widget stays as the cast indicator in the opened space
     (:160-172); see
     step 2 for not re-flashing it.

     2. Stop the drop-indicator churn

     - Today the .pm-drop-gap widget is re-created on each gap change, re-firing
     the 0.16s
     fade-in (visual churn). Change to reposition the existing widget when the
     gap index
     changes instead of recreating it (only create/destroy on drag start/end).
     Implementation:
     keep a reference to the gap widget; on gap change, move it in the DOM
     rather than rebuild +
     re-animate. Drop the per-move pm-drop-gap-in animation (or fire it once on
     first appear).

     3. FLIP settle on drop (the "glide into place")

     - In onUp, before committing the move: record each affected block's
     bounding rect (FIRST).
     - Commit buildMoveTransaction (the DOM reorders ‚ÄĒ LAST).
     - In the next frame: for each affected block, compute delta = first ‚ąí last,
     set
     transform: translateY(delta) with no transition (INVERT), then on the
     following frame
     clear the transform with the ~200ms transition (PLAY) ‚ÄĒ the blocks glide
     from their old to
     new positions. This is the same FLIP technique SortableJS uses.
     - This can REPLACE the flashDrop pulse (the glide itself confirms the drop)
     or run
     alongside it ‚ÄĒ recommend replacing the moved-block flash with the FLIP
     glide and keeping a
     subtle neighbor flash, or dropping the flash entirely for a cleaner result.
     Decide during
     implementation by feel.

     4. Tuning constants

     - Extract the displacement distance (64px), the animation duration
     (~200ms), and the easing
     into named constants at the top of the file so the feel is one-knob tunable
     (mirrors the
     rundown's single :animation="200").

     Files

     -
     disaffected-ui/src/components/content-editor/prosemirror/BlockDragHandle.js
     ‚ÄĒ the only
     file. CSS in STYLE_TEXT (:66-198), applyDisplacement (:518), onUp/drop path
     (~:455-493), flashDrop (:541), startDrag (:420 ‚ÄĒ add/remove the
     pm-dragging-active will-change class).

     Out of scope (explicitly not doing now)

     - Auto-scroll while dragging near edges (separate todo; indicators exist
     but unwired).
     - Multi-select drag (dragging several selected blocks at once).
     - Switching to a thin insertion-line visual (kept the open-gap style;
     trivial follow-up if
     the gap still feels heavy after the transform/FLIP change).
     - Replacing the whole plugin with vuedraggable (can't ‚ÄĒ the editor is a
     ProseMirror doc, not
     a Vue v-for list; SortableJS can't own the DOM here).

     Verification

     - Open the Script editor (https://dev.showbuild.app), reorder paragraphs
     and cues by
     dragging the gutter grip / cue handle:
       - The dropline indicator stays cast in place at the gap while the blocks
     below it
     slide down smoothly (GPU, no stutter) to open the space.
       - As the drop target moves, the indicator repositions without 
     re-flashing.
       - On drop, the moved block glides into its final position (FLIP), not
     snaps.
       - Side-by-side with the Rundown panel: the motion feels comparably
     smooth.
     - Regression checks: one Ctrl+Z still reverts a reorder (single transaction
     intact); drag
     still works in collapse mode and for cues; the dragged-source glow + cursor
     ghost still
     render; markdown still round-trips (block order persists through
     save/reload).
     - cd disaffected-ui && npm run lint -- --fix clean; production build green
     (NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 npx vue-cli-service build); confirm
     the dev
     bundle recompiled.

     Coordination

     BlockDragHandle.js is in the active ProseMirror editor lane (session
     63ebeb's territory).
     Announce on the ICR relay before editing and confirm they're clear of that
     file.

‚óŹ Now the bigger change: the drop-gap widget is recreated via decorations on
  each gap change (line 357-373). To stop re-flashing AND keep the cast-in-place
  behavior, I need to stop recreating it. But it's a ProseMirror decoration
  widget keyed by pm-drop-gap-${gi} ‚ÄĒ when gi changes, the key changes, so PM
  destroys+recreates it (re-firing the animation).

  The cleanest fix within the decoration model: use a stable key so PM reuses
  the same DOM element and just repositions it. Let me change the widget key to
  be constant (not gap-indexed) so it's reused.

      563  
      564 +// FLIP settle (todo #39): capture each block's position BEFOR
          +E the reorder, run                                            
      565 +// `commitFn` (which dispatches the move, reordering the DOM),
          + then on the next                                             
      566 +// frame invert each block to its old spot (no transition) and
          + clear it so the                                              
      567 +// .pm-drag-block transition glides each block from old -> new
          + position ‚ÄĒ the same                                          
      568 +// FLIP technique SortableJS uses, so the drop reads as a smoo
          +th settle, not a snap.                                        
      569 +function flipReorder(view, commitFn) {                        
      570 +  // FIRST: snapshot current top of every block element.      
      571 +  const before = new Map();                                   
      572 +  const blocksBefore = topLevelBlocks(view.state.doc);        
      573 +  for (const b of blocksBefore) {                             
      574 +    const dom = view.nodeDOM(b.start);                        
      575 +    const el = dom && dom.nodeType === 1 ? dom : (dom && dom.p
          +arentElement);                                                
      576 +    if (el && el.getBoundingClientRect) before.set(el, el.getB
          +oundingClientRect().top);                                     
      577 +  }                                                           
      578 +                                                              
      579 +  commitFn(); // LAST: the doc reorders; DOM moves to final po
          +sitions.                                                      
      580 +                                                              
      581 +  requestAnimationFrame(() => {                               
      582 +    const blocksAfter = topLevelBlocks(view.state.doc);       
      583 +    const movers = [];                                        
      584 +    for (const b of blocksAfter) {                            
      585 +      const dom = view.nodeDOM(b.start);                      
      586 +      const el = dom && dom.nodeType === 1 ? dom : (dom && dom
          +.parentElement);                                              
      587 +      if (!el || !el.getBoundingClientRect) continue;         
      588 +      const oldTop = before.get(el);                          
      589 +      if (oldTop == null) continue;                           
      590 +      const delta = oldTop - el.getBoundingClientRect().top;  
      591 +      if (!delta) continue;                                   
      592 +      // INVERT: jump back to the old position with no animati
          +on.                                                           
      593 +      el.classList.add('pm-flip-no-anim');                    
      594 +      el.style.transform = `translateY(${delta}px)`;          
      595 +      movers.push(el);                                        
      596 +    }                                                         
      597 +    if (!movers.length) return;                               
      598 +    // PLAY: next frame, drop the no-anim flag + clear the tra
          +nsform so the                                                 
      599 +    // .pm-drag-block transition animates each block to its re
          +al (new) spot.                                                
      600 +    requestAnimationFrame(() => {                             
      601 +      for (const el of movers) {                              
      602 +        el.classList.remove('pm-flip-no-anim');               
      603 +        el.style.transform = '';                              
      604 +      }                                                       
      605 +      // Clean up will-change after the settle completes.     
      606 +      setTimeout(() => { for (const el of movers) el.style.tra
          +nsform = ''; }, DRAG_ANIM_MS + 50);                           
      607 +    });                                                       
      608 +  });                                                         
      609 +}

‚óŹ All edits done. There's a potential issue I should check: applyDisplacement
  clears pm-shift-down at drop, but the FLIP runs right after ‚ÄĒ if a shifted
  block still has the inline transform from FLIP while also having a class
  transition, they could conflict. The FLIP uses inline style.transform (higher
  specificity than the class), and applyDisplacement(null) removes pm-shift-down
  before FLIP captures rects. Order in onUp: applyDisplacement(null) clears
  shifts ‚Üí then flipReorder captures (post-clear) rects ‚Üí commits ‚Üí glides.
  That's correct sequencing.

  One concern: the ghost clone strips pm-displace-after/before (old class names)
  at line 396 ‚ÄĒ now stale. Let me update that to strip the new class.

‚óŹ Done ‚ÄĒ committed (e35feeb) and live on dev. The script-editor drag animation
  is reworked per the plan.

  What changed (all in BlockDragHandle.js)

  1. Displacement: margins ‚Üí GPU transforms. The old margin-top/bottom: 64px
  (which forced a layout reflow every frame ‚ÄĒ the source of the jank) is
  replaced with transform: translateY(). Per your clarification: the dropline 
  indicator stays cast in place, and every block at/below the gap slides down
  (pm-shift-down) to open the drop space ‚ÄĒ the rows move out of the indicator's
  way. Tuned to 200ms / cubic-bezier(.2,0,0,1) to match the rundown's
  :animation="200" (the old 0.32s springy overshoot was part of the rough feel).
  2. Drop indicator no longer re-flashes. It used a gap-indexed decoration key,
  so ProseMirror destroyed+recreated it on every move (re-firing the fade). Now
  it uses a stable key, so PM reuses the same element and just repositions it ‚ÄĒ
  fade-in fires once.
  3. FLIP settle on drop. Snapshot block positions before the move ‚Üí reorder ‚Üí
  invert each block to its old spot ‚Üí play, so the moved block glides into place
  instead of snapping (the SortableJS technique). I dropped the heavy
  moved-block flash since the glide itself confirms the drop, kept a subtle
  neighbor tint.
  4. Tuning constants (DRAG_SHIFT_PX, DRAG_ANIM_MS, DRAG_EASE) at the top for
  one-knob feel adjustment, plus a will-change hint scoped to active drags only.

  The single-transaction move (one Ctrl+Z undo) and markdown round-trip are
  untouched. Lint + build green. Coordinated on the relay (announced before
  editing).

  Test it on https://dev.showbuild.app: drag a paragraph or cue ‚ÄĒ the blocks
  below the dropline should slide down smoothly, the indicator shouldn't
  re-flash, and the dropped block should glide into place. Compare to the
  rundown panel for feel.

  If the slide distance, speed, or easing needs tweaking after you try it, those
  are now single constants I can adjust in seconds. How does it feel?

‚ÄĽ recap: We're improving the Show-Build script editor; I just reworked the drag-and-drop animation (GPU transforms plus a FLIP settle) and it's committed and live on dev. Next: you test the drag feel on dev.showbuild.app and tell me if the speed or distance needs tuning.

  serve a stale bundle. You'd need a hard reload.

‚óŹ That's the key clue: "everything below it pops, but nothing animates." The
  blocks do shift (so pm-shift-down is applying), but they pop instantly instead
  of animating ‚ÄĒ meaning the CSS transition isn't firing.

  I know exactly why now. Look at the sequence in applyDisplacement + how it's
  called:
  - The displacement class is toggled on the persistent block DOM via nodeDOM.
  - But the original code had a critical detail (the old comment at line¬†334):
  doing it via decoration re-renders the element, killing the transition. The
  class is applied imperatively to avoid that.
  - The problem: my transition is on .pm-drag-block, but the element ProseMirror
  re-renders (on the setDrag meta dispatch that fires right before
  applyDisplacement in onMove) gets a fresh DOM node ‚ÄĒ so toggling the class on
  it has no "from" state to transition from. The transform jumps with no
  animation = "pops."

  The old margin version had the same structural issue but the comment claimed
  it worked. Let me verify the actual flow ‚ÄĒ specifically whether setDrag causes
  a decoration redraw that swaps the DOM element before applyDisplacement runs.

‚óŹ I see the exact bug. In onMove:
  1. setDrag(...) dispatches a transaction ‚Üí this rebuilds the decorations
  (buildDecorations), which re-applies the pm-drag-source node decoration etc. A
  node decoration change can cause ProseMirror to recreate the block's DOM 
  node.
  2. Then requestAnimationFrame(() => applyDisplacement(...)) toggles
  pm-shift-down on the (possibly brand-new) element.

  If the element was just recreated, there's no prior render with transform: 
  translateY(0) to animate from ‚ÄĒ the new element is born, then immediately gets
  the class ‚Üí it pops to the shifted position with no transition.

  The old margin code "worked" per the comment, but you're telling me it also 
  popped ("nothing animates... no different than before") ‚ÄĒ so honestly the 
  transition was likely never firing in either version. The comment was
  aspirational.

  The real fix: ensure the block elements have a stable transition baseline. The
  issue is the decoration rebuild swapping DOM. The cleanest fix: don't toggle
  the class right after a decoration-dispatch on a maybe-new element ‚ÄĒ instead,
  set the transform via inline style on a stable reference, OR (better) decouple
  displacement from the decoration redraw entirely.

  Let me verify whether the DOM element is actually being recreated by checking
  if pm-drag-source (a node decoration) is what forces it. The fact that
  everything "pops" cleanly (just no animation) means the class does apply ‚ÄĒ so
  the element reference is fine, but the transition isn't engaging. The classic
  cause: applying a class in the same frame the element first appears, or the
  element has transition: none from somewhere.

  Let me check: is there a global CSS or the pm-flip-no-anim leaking, or does
  the ProseMirror redraw strip+reapply removing the transition mid-flight? The
  most robust fix is to force the transition to engage by setting the transform
  with a guaranteed reflow between the baseline and the change. Let me
  restructure applyDisplacement to do that.

‚ĚĮ the block's too shifter, they have always shifted 

‚óŹ That confirms it precisely: the blocks have always shifted (popped) ‚ÄĒ they've 
  never animated. So the transition has never worked, in either the old margin
  version or my new transform version. The "rough" feel is this instant pop with
  no slide.

  The root cause is what I identified: the setDrag dispatch in onMove rebuilds
  the decorations, which swaps the block's DOM element, so when
  applyDisplacement toggles the class on the new element there's no "from" state
  ‚Üí instant jump, no transition.

  The fix: decouple the displacement animation from the decoration redraw.
  Instead of relying on a persistent element that keeps getting swapped, I'll
  set the transform directly on the live element and force the browser to
  register a baseline before the change so the transition engages ‚ÄĒ but more
  importantly, I should stop the decoration rebuild from churning the element on
  every gap change. Let me look at what's actually causing the DOM swap. The
  pm-drag-source node decoration is constant during a drag (same sourceIndex),
  so it shouldn't swap. But the drop-gap widget decoration changing position
  triggers a DecorationSet rebuild.

  Let me trace whether the block DOM is genuinely being recreated. Quickest
  definitive test: log whether the same element reference persists across moves.
  But I can reason it: a Decoration.node whose attrs don't change shouldn't
  recreate the DOM. The displacement class isn't in decorations (it's
  imperative). So the element should be stable... which means the transition
  should fire. Yet it doesn't.

  The likely real culprit: requestAnimationFrame timing + the transition needs 
  the element to first render at translateY(0). When the class is added, the
  element is already at its natural position (transform none ‚Čą translateY(0)),
  and adding pm-shift-down sets translateY(64px) ‚ÄĒ that should transition.
  Unless the element is fresh. Let me actually check if ProseMirror recreates
  node DOM on meta-only transactions by examining how decorations diff.

  Let me stop theorizing and instrument it directly to see the truth.

‚óŹ Let me check the plugin's decorations() and apply() to understand the redraw,
  and whether view.nodeDOM returns a stable element. The cleanest path forward,
  given the blocks have never animated: rather than fight the decoration-redraw
  timing, I'll drive the displacement transition entirely with inline transforms
  set at the right moment, and crucially apply them synchronously without the 
  rAF-after-dispatch indirection that's racing the redraw. But first, the real
  question is whether the element is stable. Let me look at how the plugin's
  decorations spec works (is pm-drag-source causing per-move redraws).

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Now I understand the full mechanism. The decorations(state) returns
  buildDecorations on every transaction ‚ÄĒ and each setDrag dispatch produces a
  new transaction, so decorations rebuild. The pm-drag-source class is a
  Decoration.node ‚ÄĒ when the DecorationSet changes (the drop-gap widget moves),
  ProseMirror re-renders the node decorations, which re-applies the class attrs 
  to the DOM, wiping any imperative pm-shift-down class that applyDisplacement
  added (because node decorations set the full class list, overwriting).

  That's the real bug. The decoration redraw resets the element's class to just
  ['pm-drag-block', 'pm-drag-gutter', maybe 'pm-drag-source'] ‚ÄĒ stripping 
  pm-shift-down ‚ÄĒ then applyDisplacement's rAF re-adds it. So the element goes:
  shifted ‚Üí (redraw strips it ‚Üí unshifted) ‚Üí rAF re-adds shifted. The
  strip+readd in quick succession means no smooth transition, and worse, it
  fights itself = "pops."

  The clean fix: make displacement part of the node decoration itself, not
  imperative. Put pm-shift-down into the decoration class list in
  buildDecorations based on the gap index, so the redraw applies it consistently
  and the transform animates from the element's stable rendered state.
  ProseMirror node decorations on an unchanged node update attrs in place (don't
  recreate the DOM), so the transition fires.

  Let me rewrite: remove applyDisplacement entirely, and compute the shift class
  inside buildDecorations.

  (computed from dragState.gapIndex), so the redraw applies it consistently and
  ProseMirror updates the class in-place (which animates). Let me rewrite this
  block.

‚óŹ Clean. Now there's one more thing to verify: does ProseMirror actually update
  a node decoration's class in place (not recreate the DOM)? If it recreates the
  DOM on class change, we're back to popping. The way to guarantee in-place
  updating is that the node itself is unchanged and only the decoration attrs
  differ ‚ÄĒ ProseMirror's view diffing updates attrs in place in that case. This
  should work.

  But I'm also now worried about a different angle: the user said "everything
  below pops" ‚ÄĒ meaning all blocks below shifted, which matches my i >= gapIndex
  logic (good, that's the intended behavior). The issue is purely the

‚óŹ Committed (680a5d6).

  What I found ‚ÄĒ and the honest take

  Your observation nailed it: the blocks never animated ‚ÄĒ they popped ‚ÄĒ in both 
  the old version and my first attempt. So my first commit changed the technique
  but not the visible result, because the actual animation was never firing in
  the first place. That's why you saw "no change."

  The real root cause: the displacement class was being toggled imperatively on
  the block DOM, but every pointer-move dispatches a transaction that rebuilds 
  ProseMirror's node decorations ‚ÄĒ and that rebuild re-applies the decoration's 
  class list, stripping the pm-shift-down class, then a requestAnimationFrame
  re-adds it. That strip-then-readd on every move destroyed the CSS transition's
  start state ‚Üí instant pop, no slide.

  The fix: I moved pm-shift-down into the decoration class list (computed from
  the gap index in buildDecorations). Now ProseMirror updates the class in place
  on the same DOM node, so the transform has a stable from‚Üíto and the
  transition actually fires.

  Please test (CTL+F5 to hard-reload dev)

  Grab a paragraph and drag ‚ÄĒ the blocks below should now slide down smoothly to
  open the gap, instead of popping.

  I want to be straight with you: I'm fairly confident this is the right fix,
  but I can't see your screen, and ProseMirror's decoration-diffing behavior has
  edge cases. If it still pops after a hard reload, that tells me PM is
  recreating the DOM node on the class change (not updating in place) ‚ÄĒ and I'll
  switch to a different approach (a ProseMirror node view or a dedicated
  displacement layer that's immune to the decoration rebuild).

  So: does it slide now, or still pop?

‚óŹ Done ‚ÄĒ committed (ac1c284) and live on dev.

  What I fixed (#46)

  The regression was exactly as I suspected ‚ÄĒ I caused it. In the animation
  rework I swapped the full flashDrop() (dropped block 3√ó + both neighbors 2√ó)
  for a neighbors-only flashNeighbors(), on the assumption the FLIP glide would
  stand in for the dropped-block flash. You wanted the flash back, so I restored
  it:

  - Dropped block: flashes 3√ó fast in the drag color (pm-flash-drop).
  - Above + below neighbors: flash 2√ó slower at half the opacity ‚ÄĒ I changed the
  neighbor tint from the full ~0.15 alpha to ~0.075 so it reads as a lighter
  echo, per your spec.
  - Fired ~90ms after the move (so the moved block's fresh DOM exists and is
  re-queried), and it coexists with the FLIP glide (flash uses
  background+box-shadow, glide uses transform ‚ÄĒ no conflict).

  The honest caveat

  Your original report said the target specifically wasn't flashing even though
  the neighbors were. Restoring flashDrop should fix it ‚ÄĒ but if the underlying
  reason was a DOM-staleness/timing issue (the moved block gets fresh DOM and a
  redraw wipes the class), it's possible the target still won't flash and I'll
  need to adjust the timing. I can't confirm that without seeing it.

  Please CTL+F5 and drop a paragraph and a cue, both next to a paragraph and 
  next to a cue, and tell me: does the dropped block now flash 3√ó (in all four
  cases), and do the neighbors flash at a visibly lighter/half intensity? If the
  target still doesn't flash in any case, that tells me it's the timing issue
  and I'll fix the DOM re-query.

‚ÄĽ recap: Goal: smoothing the script editor's drag-and-drop (todo #39) and restoring its drop-flash (#46), both committed and live on dev. Next: you hard-reload (CTL+F5) and confirm the dropped block flashes 3√ó and neighbors flash lighter.

  class even landing on the paragraph <p>? Let me check blockElAt/nodeDOM ‚ÄĒ for

‚óŹ No obvious CSS conflict. The flash class lands on the <p> and the keyframe
  sets background. So why don't paragraphs flash but cues do?

  The key difference: cues render via a NodeView (a Vue component with its own 
  card DOM that has a visible background), while paragraphs are bare <p> with
  background: transparent and 0.15-alpha flash that's barely perceptible on a 
  <p>. But you say cue neighbors flash visibly and paragraph targets/neighbors

‚óŹ That's the answer, and it rules out everything I was worried about:

  - ‚úÖ moved: is a <P>, connected=true (not stale)
  - ‚úÖ The class is added (+pm-flash-drop)
  - ‚úÖ animationName = pm-flash-drop ‚ÄĒ the keyframe is computing and running

  So the animation is firing on the paragraph. The problem is purely visual: you
  can't see it. The keyframe animates background-color to DRAGLIGHT
  (rgba(33,150,243,0.15)) ‚ÄĒ a 15%-alpha blue ‚ÄĒ but on a <p> paragraph that flash
  is too faint to perceive, especially since each pulse is only 0.2s and the
  peak (50% keyframe) passes in ~0.1s. Cue cards show it because the card's
  solid background + border makes even a faint tint visible.

  The fix is simple: make the flash actually visible on a bare paragraph ‚ÄĒ
  stronger color, and use the box-shadow ring (which the drop keyframe already
  has but the alpha is the limiter). Let me bump the flash intensity. Let me
  look at the exact keyframe values.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ The issue is clear. The drop keyframe's box-shadow: 0 0 0 3px DROPLINE should
  be visible (solid blue ring) ‚ÄĒ but DROPLINE is var(--dropline-color, 
  rgb(33,150,243)), full opacity. So the ring should show. Yet you don't see it
  on paragraphs.

  The likely culprit: box-shadow on a <p> that has no padding gets 
  clipped/hidden, or the paragraph's box is the full editor width and the shadow
  blends in. And the background at 0.15 alpha is genuinely too faint. The
  robust fix: make the drop flash clearly visible with a stronger background
  (raise alpha) and ensure the box-shadow ring renders. Let me strengthen both
  keyframes ‚ÄĒ drop gets a much more visible tint + ring, neighbors get half of
  that (so still visible, just lighter).

‚óŹ Done ‚ÄĒ committed (fb4489d) and live on dev. Diagnostic logging removed.

  The diagnosis (now certain, not guessed)

  Your console output was the smoking gun and cleared the bug entirely: the
  flash was firing on paragraphs all along ‚ÄĒ
  - the class was added (+pm-flash-drop),
  - on the correct connected <P> element,
  - and animationName = pm-flash-drop confirmed the keyframe was actually
  running.

  So it wasn't broken ‚ÄĒ it was invisible. The flash tinted the background at
  rgba(33,150,243,**0.15**) ‚ÄĒ a 15%-alpha blue that's imperceptible on a bare
  <p>. Cue cards showed it only because their solid card background + border
  made even that faint tint visible. That's exactly why "only the cue neighbor
  flashed."

  The fix

  Cranked the visibility:
  - Dropped block: fill raised to ~0.45 alpha + the solid dropline ring (3√ó
  fast).
  - Neighbors: ~0.225 = exactly half the drop's opacity, per your spec (2√ó
  slower, no ring).

  Both rounded for a clean shape.

  CTL+F5 and drop a paragraph next to other paragraphs ‚ÄĒ you should now clearly
  see the dropped block flash 3√ó bright, with the above/below neighbors flashing
  at half intensity. If 0.45 is too strong/weak, that's a one-number tweak (the
  dropflash-color alpha).

  Once you confirm it's visible, #46 is done and I'll mark it complete.

‚ĚĮ still zero flashing                                                           

‚óŹ Still zero ‚ÄĒ and the diagnostic proved the animation runs and the class lands.
  So something is painting over or clipping the flash on the <p>. The most
  likely culprit: the <p> paragraphs have their own background (e.g. a
  speaker-colored background, or the editor sets background on paragraphs) that
  sits on top, OR the box has zero painted area. The console showed classes like
  P.asian-scott / P.josh ‚ÄĒ those speaker classes may set a background that
  overrides the flash.

  Let me check what CSS the speaker-classed paragraphs carry ‚ÄĒ specifically
  backgrounds.

‚óŹ The speaker classes don't appear to set a paragraph background directly. But
  there's a SpeakerHeaders.js plugin adding decorations, and the paragraph
  styling is complex. Rather than keep hunting through CSS, let me think about
  what the diagnostic didn't rule out: the animation runs, but the <p>'s painted
  background area might be covered, or the background-color on the <p> is being
  composited under the text/line content.

  Actually ‚ÄĒ a stronger hypothesis just occurred to me. The flashDrop keyframe
  animates background-color, but !important was on the animation property, not 
  the background. If another rule sets background-color on the <p> with higher
  specificity (or !important), the keyframe's background gets overridden. But
  the diagnostic showed animationName=pm-flash-drop, meaning the animation is
  assigned... yet an animated property can still be overridden by an !important
  declaration.

  Let me check the live computed style. But I can't inspect your browser. The
  fastest definitive test: instead of background-color (which may be
  overridden/invisible), use an outline or a box-shadow with !important that
  can't be covered ‚ÄĒ outline paints on top and ignores background stacking. Let
  me also check: is there a global transition on p that's smoothing the 0.2s
  flash into invisibility? Let me look at the ProseMirror paragraph base CSS in
  ScriptEditor.

‚óŹ The base .ProseMirror p has no background (just margin/font). So the flash's
  background-color should paint. No competing background, no transition
  smoothing it. The diagnostic confirmed the animation runs. Yet you see
  nothing.

  This is genuinely puzzling, which means I'm missing something the diagnostic
  didn't capture. Let me reconsider what the console actually told me:
  cs.animationName=pm-flash-drop confirms the animation property is set, but it 
  does NOT confirm the animation actually played/painted. If the element's
  animation-play-state is paused, or animation-duration resolved to 0, or the
  keyframe's properties don't differ from the base...

  Actually ‚ÄĒ here's a strong new hypothesis. The flash classes are applied at
  the 90ms mark, but the decoration redraw that happens continuously could be
  re-rendering the <p> and stripping the flash class right after I add it (the
  same root cause as the displacement bug!). The displacement fix moved
  pm-shift-down into the decoration. But pm-flash-drop is added imperatively in
  flashDrop ‚ÄĒ so a post-drop decoration redraw would strip it, exactly like
  before. That's why animationName showed correct at the instant of the log
  (right after adding), but the class gets wiped on the next redraw before it
  can paint.

  Let me verify there's a redraw after the move. The move dispatch +
  setDrag({active:false}) both trigger buildDecorations, which sets the node
  class list (without the flash class) ‚Üí strips pm-flash-drop. Let me confirm
  and fix by extending the diagnostic to check if the class survives ‚ÄĒ actually,
  let me just fix it the same way as displacement: I can't easily put a
  transient flash in decorations. Better: apply the flash via inline style/a
  wrapper, OR re-apply after the redraw settles. The cleanest robust fix: apply
  the flash on a longer delay (after all redraws settle) AND re-add if stripped.
  Let me check the timing by testing whether a longer delay works.

  Let me look at how flashes were handled originally vs now, and just make the
  flash survive redraws by using requestAnimationFrame to confirm it persists.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ The sequence in onUp:
  1. setDrag({active:false}) ‚Üí redraw #1 (strips any flash, but none yet)
  2. flipReorder(() => view.dispatch(moveTr)) ‚Üí the move ‚Üí redraw #2
  3. setTimeout(flashDrop, 90) ‚Üí adds flash class

  So flash is added at 90ms, after the redraws. But the
  pm-drag-source/decoration redraws happen before the flash, so the flash class
  added at 90ms should survive... unless there's a later redraw (e.g. the FLIP's
  rAF cleanup, or an autosave-triggered re-render, or the speaker-header plugin
  re-decorating).

  I've been theorizing too much. Let me get definitive proof with a better
  diagnostic: re-add the log but also check the class is still present after a 
  delay (did a redraw strip it?), and check animation-play-state + whether it's
  actually visible by reading the computed background mid-animation. Let me put
  a sharper probe in.

‚óŹ There it is ‚ÄĒ the smoking gun:

  [flash@50ms] pm-flash-drop hasClass= true bg=  animName=  playState=
  connected= FALSE
  [flash@250ms] pm-flash-drop hasClass= true connected= FALSE

  connected= **false** ‚ÄĒ the element I'm flashing is detached from the DOM. The
  class is on it, but it's an orphaned node, so nothing paints. And
  bg=/animName= are empty because getComputedStyle on a disconnected element
  returns nothing.

  Why: the move (buildMoveTransaction = delete + re-insert the node) makes
  ProseMirror build a brand-new DOM element for the moved block. My
  blockElAt(view, landedIndex) is resolving the old, detached element (or the
  FLIP captured a reference that got replaced). So I flash a ghost node that
  isn't on screen.

  The cue worked because cue NodeViews often reuse their DOM across moves; the
  bare <p> gets recreated.

  The fix: re-query the element fresh at flash time from the current
  view.nodeDOM (which I'm doing via blockElAt) ‚ÄĒ but the issue is nodeDOM is
  returning stale DOM at 90ms because the view hasn't fully reconciled. I need
  to flash after the view's DOM is current, and verify isConnected before
  flashing, re-resolving if not. Let me fix flashDrop to grab the live connected
  element.
  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ The root cause is clear: at flash time, view.nodeDOM for the moved block
  returns a detached element. This is because the autosave/serialization
  round-trip (visible in the logs ‚ÄĒ flushPendingChanges ‚Üí updateScriptContent
  fires during flipReorder) causes the editor to re-render the whole document,
  replacing the DOM nodes after my flash.

  The robust fix: don't rely on a stale element reference or a fixed delay.
  Instead, flash on a requestAnimationFrame after the view settles, and guard
  with isConnected ‚ÄĒ if the resolved element isn't connected, re-resolve via
  nodeDOM (which will return the new live node once the view reconciles). Let me
  rewrite the flash to resolve+verify the live element, retrying briefly if
  detached.

‚óŹ Committed (9bb1596).

  The diagnosis (definitive, from your console)

  Your log was the answer:
  [flash@50ms] pm-flash-drop hasClass=true bg= animName= playState=
  connected=FALSE
  connected=false ‚ÄĒ I was applying the flash class to a detached DOM node. The
  class was there, but the element wasn't on screen, so nothing painted.
  (bg/animName were empty because getComputedStyle returns nothing for a
  disconnected element.)

  Why: the move (delete + re-insert the block) plus the autosave round-trip
  (visible in your log ‚ÄĒ flushPendingChanges ‚Üí updateScriptContent fired during
  the drop) re-renders the document, replacing the <p>'s DOM node. My flash
  captured the old, now-detached node. Cue cards worked because their NodeView
  DOM survives a move; a bare <p> gets recreated. That's exactly why "only the
  cue neighbor flashed."

  The fix

  The flash now resolves the element fresh by index via view.nodeDOM at fire
  time, and ‚ÄĒ crucially ‚ÄĒ checks isConnected. If the live node isn't ready yet
  (the view is still reconciling after the re-render), it retries on the next 
  animation frame (up to ~8 frames) until the real, on-screen node exists, then
  flashes that.

  I removed the temp diagnostics and the now-unused helper.

  CTL+F5 and drop a paragraph next to other paragraphs. This time the flash
  should land on the actual visible block ‚ÄĒ you should see the dropped block
  flash 3√ó and the neighbors at half intensity. This was the genuine root cause
  (detached node), so I'm more confident now ‚ÄĒ but please confirm, and if it's
  still not showing, the retry count or timing is the last knob.

‚ĚĮ still not flashing.  if i drop it next to the cue, the cue flashes.  but not

‚óŹ Still no paragraph flash ‚ÄĒ but cues flash. That's the critical signal: cues
  and paragraphs render differently, and my whole theory (detached node) was
  about timing. But if cues consistently flash and paragraphs never do, the
  difference isn't timing ‚ÄĒ it's the element itself. A cue's flashable DOM has a
  visible background (the card); a paragraph <p> apparently does not show the
  animated background at all, even when connected.

  I've been wrong twice. Let me do the ICR check-in, then stop theorizing and
  directly inspect the live paragraph element ‚ÄĒ its actual computed background
  during the flash ‚ÄĒ by checking what the <p> DOM actually is and whether
  ProseMirror wraps it.

‚óŹ ICR: nothing actionable. The diagnostic earlier already proved the <p> is the
  element (P.asian-scott), it got the class, animation pm-flash-drop was
  assigned. The only failure was connected=false (timing). My retry fix should
  handle the connected issue. But you say it still doesn't flash ‚ÄĒ and
  crucially, cues do.

  Let me re-examine my CSS scoping. My flash CSS is injected via the plugin's
  STYLE_TEXT as .ProseMirror .pm-flash-drop. But ScriptEditor.vue's paragraph
  styles are scoped under .script-editor-host :deep(.ProseMirror p). The <p>
  lives under both. The flash background sets background-color on the
  .ProseMirror .pm-flash-drop ‚ÄĒ but if any scoped rule sets background on
  .ProseMirror p with equal-or-higher specificity, it wins (animations are
  overridden by !important declarations, and :deep rules can have high
  specificity).

  Actually ‚ÄĒ the real difference between cue and paragraph: the cue's flashable 
  element is the NodeView card (which the .ProseMirror .pm-flash-drop background
  paints cleanly on), while the <p> is a text-line element where the background
  might paint behind the text only and be invisible at 0.2s, OR a scoped rule 
  overrides it.

  Let me add a brute-force diagnostic that's impossible to miss: instead of
  trusting the keyframe, directly set an inline outline (which can't be
  overridden by background rules and paints on top) on the live paragraph during
  flash, and log the computed background. This will tell us definitively
  whether (a) the element paints at all and (b) whether background specifically
  is being suppressed.

  You didn't paste the log line, and I need it. But let me reason about the most

‚ĚĮ BlockDragHandle.js:637 [flashDIAG] el= P.josh pm-drag-block pm-drag-gut       
  connected= true rect= 670x248 bgFromClass= rgba(0, 0, 0, 0) animName=

‚óŹ Now I have it ‚ÄĒ and it's contradictory in a revealing way:

  el= P.josh ... connected= TRUE  rect= 670x248  bgFromClass= rgba(0,0,0,0)
  animName= pm-flash-drop

  - ‚úÖ Element is a real <p>, connected=true, 248px tall (definitely
  painted/visible)
  - ‚úÖ animName= pm-flash-drop (animation assigned)
  - ‚ĚĆ bgFromClass= rgba(0,0,0,0) ‚ÄĒ transparent, the flash bg isn't computing
  - ‚ĚĆ And you saw NO magenta ‚ÄĒ even though I set outline + background-color
  with !important inline

  That last point is the key. A 248px-tall connected element with !important
  inline magenta outline+bg that's invisible can only mean one thing: I'm 
  setting the styles on this element, but it gets immediately 
  overwritten/replaced. The autosave re-render (which fires on drop ‚ÄĒ we saw
  updateScriptContent‚ÜíflushPendingChanges in your earlier log) recreates the <p>
  DOM node right after I style it, so both my inline styles AND the class get
  blown away before paint. The element I logged is connected at that instant but
  replaced milliseconds later.

  So the real culprit: the drop triggers a full document re-render (via the 
  autosave serialization round-trip), which rebuilds every paragraph's DOM,
  wiping any flash. This is the same class of problem as the displacement bug ‚ÄĒ
  and the fix is the same: the flash must survive the re-render, which means it 
  can't be a transient class/style on a recreated element.

‚óŹ Committed (ac96b24).

  The conclusive diagnosis

  Your tests nailed it through elimination:
  - The <p> was connected, 248px tall, had the class, animName=pm-flash-drop ‚úď
  - Yet even an !important inline magenta outline+background was invisible

  The only thing that explains "I set !important inline styles on a connected,
  sized element and nothing shows" is: the element gets replaced milliseconds 
  later. The drop triggers an immediate autosave serialization round-trip that 
  re-renders the paragraph DOM, rebuilding the <p> ‚ÄĒ wiping any class or inline
  style I'd put on the old one. Cue cards kept flashing the whole time because
  their NodeView DOM survives a move; bare <p>s get recreated.

  The real fix

  I moved the flash off imperative classes and into the ProseMirror decorations
  ‚ÄĒ the exact same approach that made the displacement animation work (which you
  confirmed does animate). Now:
  - After the move, I set flash: {landed} in the plugin state.
  - buildDecorations applies pm-flash-drop to the landed block and
  pm-flash-neighbor to its neighbors.
  - It clears after ~750ms.

  Because ProseMirror re-applies decoration classes on every redraw, the flash
  survives the autosave re-render and actually paints. Diagnostics + the
  imperative flash code are removed.

  CTL+F5 and drop a paragraph next to other paragraphs. This should finally show
  the dropped block flashing 3√ó with neighbors at half intensity ‚ÄĒ because it's
  now immune to the re-render that was killing it.

  I'm genuinely more confident this time: it's the same mechanism that's already
  proven to work for the displacement on these same paragraphs. But you've been
  right every time so far ‚ÄĒ does it flash now?

  block 3√ó with neighbors at half intensity. Both #39 (smooth animation) and #46
  (flash) are complete.

  The lesson worth keeping

  The recurring trap in this editor: anything you set imperatively on a 
  paragraph's DOM (class or inline style) gets wiped when ProseMirror re-renders
  it ‚ÄĒ and a drop triggers an immediate autosave round-trip that does exactly
  that. The fix, both for displacement and the flash, was the same: drive it 
  through ProseMirror decorations, which get re-applied on every redraw. Cue
  cards masked the bug because their NodeView DOM survives. That's a useful
  pattern for any future per-block visual effect in this editor.

  (coordinating with 1ed2f6), or keep going with something else first?
