‚óŹ Found it. The deployed bundle shows the shift CSS as the literal 
  uninterpolated string:

  pm-shift-down { transform: translateY(${DRAG_SHIFT_PX / 2}

  The ${DRAG_SHIFT_PX / 2} template-literal expression is showing up literally
  in the output instead of being computed to 32px. That means the STYLE_TEXT
  template literal got broken ‚ÄĒ a stray backtick somewhere closed the template
  string early, so everything after it (including the shift transforms) is no
  longer inside the template literal and the ${} isn't interpolated. So
  .pm-shift-down gets an invalid translateY(${DRAG_SHIFT_PX / 2}) ‚Üí no transform
  ‚Üí pop.

  breaking the template literal.

  If it still pops after a hard reload, set window.__PM_DRAG_DEBUG = true and

  intermediate fractional values (y19.0, y-12.5) the glide is working and it's a
  perception issue; if they jump straight 0‚Üí32 with trans=transform,opacity
  then something genuinely regressed and I'll dig in with fresh data.

  ---
  Now, your actual question ‚ÄĒ is the drag saving and is it undoable? Yes to 
  both:

  Saving: ‚úÖ The reorder dispatches a real document transaction
  (buildMoveTransaction ‚Üí delete the block + re-insert it at the new spot).
  That's a doc change, so ScriptEditor's onUpdate fires ‚Üí scheduleSave() ‚Üí
  serializes the reordered doc to markdown ‚Üí emits update:scriptContent ‚Üí the
  parent's autosave persists it to the database (the standard ~1.5s debounced
  save path). The drag preview transactions (the live displacement) are
  explicitly flagged addToHistory: false and don't count as content changes ‚ÄĒ
  only the final committed move saves.

  Undoable: ‚úÖ The move transaction is dispatched without addToHistory: false,
  so it enters ProseMirror's undo history as a single step. One Ctrl+Z reverts
  the whole reorder (delete + insert are in one transaction = one undo). The
  drag preview/displacement transactions are kept out of history, so undo jumps
  straight back to the pre-drag order without stepping through intermediate
  previews.

  So: drag a block, it saves to the DB, and Ctrl+Z puts it back. Confirm the
  glide after a hard reload and I'll finish up (strip debug, set final bar
  speed, commit).

  Those fractional sweeps (0 ‚Üí 26 ‚Üí 31 ‚Üí 32, and 0 ‚Üí ‚ąí3 ‚Üí ‚ąí28 ‚Üí ‚ąí32) are the
  transition smoothly interpolating in BOTH directions. The transition is
  transform,opacity|0.2s everywhere. The displacement is animating correctly, up
  and down. The code is at the working state ‚ÄĒ nothing needs reverting.

  So what you're seeing as a "pop" is not the paragraph displacement ‚ÄĒ that's
  gliding (proven). The pop must be a different visual element. Given everything

  like the others.
  2. The bar's height change when it appears, or
  3. The blocks near the source rearranging as the source block itself moves.

‚ĚĮ didnt we already solve this earlier in the session? 

‚óŹ You're right to ask ‚ÄĒ and yes, we partially did. Let me connect the dots
  before you re-test.

  Earlier this session we hit this exact directional-pop symptom twice and found
  two separate causes, both real:

  1. The .pm-dim override ‚ÄĒ .pm-dim { transition: opacity } was clobbering the
  transform transition. Fixed (combined transition on .pm-drag-block).
  2. The asymmetry root cause ‚ÄĒ the research confirmed: "a feature class must 
  never re-declare the transition shorthand... which class wins varies by 
  direction/state ‚ÄĒ that was the up-vs-down asymmetry." We thought fixing
  .pm-dim resolved it.

  But the console then proved both directions interpolate (we saw tf=y-3.19,
  tf=y-28.09 on UP-shifts) ‚ÄĒ meaning the up-shifted blocks DO glide. So whatever
  you're seeing pop on up-moves is a different element than the one we 
  instrumented, OR there's a second class on the up-moving blocks still
  re-declaring transition that the research warned about and we didn't fully
  sweep.

  So no ‚ÄĒ we haven't fully closed it, because we kept measuring "the first
  .pm-shift-up block" and it always looked fine. The new diagnostic I just
  deployed dumps every block at once (with its shift state, live transform-Y,
  and whether it's the grey source) ‚ÄĒ so this time we'll see which specific 
  block pops on an up-move instead of sampling one that happens to be animating
  fine.

  CTL+F5, window.__PM_DRAG_DEBUG = true, then move the bar UP a couple gaps (the
  popping direction) and paste the [pm-shift] lines. Each line is now gap=N 
  src=N followed by per-block entries like 3*U:-32 4D:32 (index, *=grey source,
  U/D=up/down shift, transform-Y).

  If a block shows up at :-32 or :32 instantly between two samples with no
  in-between value, that's the popper ‚ÄĒ and now I'll see exactly which one and
  whether it's carrying a second transition-declaring class (per the research,
  the likely culprit). This should finally pin the specific element instead of
  us circling.

‚óŹ This data is the smoking gun. Compare the two directions:

  Moving DOWN (gap 3‚Üí13) ‚ÄĒ watch block 11 as the gap passes it:
  gap=11: 10U:32  11D:32        ‚Üź block 11 is D:32
  gap=12: 11U:32  12D:32        ‚Üź block 11 flips to U:32 ... wait, U:32?!
  gap=13: 11U:6  12U:32         ‚Üź block 11: U but at +6, sweeping toward -32

  Moving UP (gap 13‚Üí3) ‚ÄĒ watch block 11:
  gap=12: 11U:-4.2     ‚Üź sweeping
  gap=11: 11D:32       ‚Üź INSTANTLY at D:32, no intermediate!

  Look at the transition between gap=12 and gap=11 on the up-move: block 11 goes
  from 11U:-4.2 straight to 11D:32 ‚ÄĒ a jump of 36px with no intermediate value.
  That's the pop.

  Here's the root cause: when a block flips from pm-shift-up (‚ąí32) to
  pm-shift-down (+32), it crosses the gap. On the down-move, the block briefly
  shows U:32 ‚ÄĒ a positive value while still tagged U (the transition hasn't
  caught up). But on the up-move, the block jumps U:-4.2 ‚Üí D:32 instantly.

  The real issue: the -32 ‚Üí +32 flip is a 64px jump, and the transition only 
  fires if the browser sees the change as continuous on the same element. When
  the class flips from pm-shift-up to pm-shift-down, the target transform
  changes from ‚ąí32 to +32. The transition SHOULD animate that 64px. On
  down-moves it does (we see U:32 intermediate). On up-moves, the block goes
  from a mid-transition value (‚ąí4.2) and the class flip resets it.

  Actually ‚ÄĒ the cleanest read: on the up-move, block 11 at gap=12 is U:-4.2
  (mid-glide, heading to ‚ąí32). Then gap becomes 11, block 11 flips to D with
  target +32. The transition from ‚ąí4.2 to +32 is 36px and SHOULD glide... but it
  shows 32 immediately next sample.

  The asymmetry is subtle but the fix the research pointed to is clear: a block
  flipping shift-up ‚ÜĒ shift-down is a transform sign-flip, and when it happens
  during an in-flight transition the browser can snap. Let me check the relay,
  then apply the robust fix ‚ÄĒ eliminate the up/down flip entirely by making the
  displacement a single continuous direction relative to the source, so no block
  ever flips sign.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚ĚĮ we fixed this by flowing the drop bar?  now? 

‚óŹ Relay clear. And you're asking a sharp question ‚ÄĒ "we fixed this by floating
  the drop bar... now?"

  Let me be precise about what the float fix did vs. didn't fix:

  - The float fix solved the BAR's reflow pop ‚ÄĒ the 52px box jumping between
  layout slots and shoving text. That's genuinely fixed (you confirmed "Good
  that is working").
  - This up-move pop is a DIFFERENT bug the data just exposed: it's the blocks 
  flipping from pm-shift-up (‚ąí32) to pm-shift-down (+32) as the gap crosses
  them. That's a 64px sign-flip, and on up-moves it snaps instead of gliding
  (block 11: U:-4.2 ‚Üí D:32 instantly, no intermediate).

  So no ‚ÄĒ floating the bar didn't fix this. This is the shift-direction flip,
  which the research actually predicted ("a transform sign-flip during an
  in-flight transition can snap").

  The clean fix: eliminate the sign-flip. Instead of "blocks above the gap shift
  ‚ąí32, blocks below shift +32" (which makes any block crossing the gap flip
  ‚ąí32‚ÜĒ+32), make the displacement one-directional relative to the source block
  so no block ever reverses sign mid-transition. Concretely: only the blocks
  between the source and the gap shift, all in the same direction (toward
  closing the source's old slot / opening the new one). A block either shifts or
  doesn't ‚ÄĒ it never flips +‚ÜĒ‚ąí.

  This is a real logic change to the displacement, not a CSS tweak. Let me
  confirm the approach before I rewrite it, since it changes how the gap opens:

‚óŹ¬†User answered Claude's questions:
  ‚éŅ ¬†¬∑ The up-move pop is blocks flipping shift direction (‚ąí32 ‚ÜĒ +32) as the gap
     crosses them ‚ÄĒ a 64px sign-flip that snaps instead of gliding. How should 
     I fix it? ‚Üí You pick

‚óŹ I'll pick. But first let me look harder at the data, because I want to make
  sure I'm fixing the real thing ‚ÄĒ the sign-flip theory needs to explain why
  DOWN works and UP doesn't, when both involve the same flip.

  Looking again:
  - DOWN-move, block 11: D:32 ‚Üí U:32 ‚Üí U:6 ‚ÄĒ when it flips to U, it's
  momentarily still at +32 then sweeps down to +6 toward ‚ąí32. The browser caught
  the ‚ąí32 target and is animating from +32. Glides.
  - UP-move, block 11: U:-4.2 ‚Üí D:32 ‚ÄĒ flips to D, target +32, and it's
  instantly at +32. Pops.

  The difference: on DOWN, the flipping block was at the fully-settled end of
  its previous shift (+32 or ‚ąí32) when it flipped. On UP, block 11 was
  mid-transition (-4.2, still animating) when it flipped.

  That's the real cause: when a block's class flips while a transition is
  already in flight, the browser snaps to the new value instead of redirecting
  the in-flight transition. CSS transitions don't always interrupt-and-redirect
  cleanly on a mid-flight target reversal ‚ÄĒ especially a sign flip.

  This isn't really directional ‚ÄĒ it's "did the flip happen mid-transition."
  Up-moves just happen to catch blocks mid-glide more often because of the
  stagger/timing.

  Given that, Option 1 (one-directional shift) is the correct, lower-risk fix ‚ÄĒ
  if no block ever flips sign, there's no mid-flight reversal to snap. It
  eliminates the root condition entirely rather than trying to force-glide a
  reversal the browser resists. Let me look at the displacement logic to
  implement it.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Now I understand the current logic: every block gets shifted ‚ÄĒ above the gap ‚Üí
  up, at/below ‚Üí down. That's why blocks flip sign as the gap crosses them.

  One-directional fix: Only shift the blocks between the source's original
  position and the gap, all in one direction:
  - If gap is below the source (gapIndex > sourceIndex): blocks from
  sourceIndex+1 to gapIndex-1 shift up (to fill where the source was, opening a
  slot at the gap). Direction: up only.
  - If gap is above the source (gapIndex < sourceIndex): blocks from gapIndex to
  sourceIndex-1 shift down. Direction: down only.

  A block is only ever in ONE of these ranges for a given gap, and as the gap
  moves, a block either is in the range (shifted) or not (zero) ‚ÄĒ but the
  direction for a given block never flips sign as long as the gap stays on the
  same side of the source. When the gap crosses the source, the side flips ‚ÄĒ but
  that's one specific transition, not every block crossing.

  Wait ‚ÄĒ that still flips when crossing the source. Let me think about the
  cleanest no-flip formulation. Actually the standard SortableJS approach: the
  dragged item's slot is "removed" and items shift to fill, opening a slot at
  the target. Let me implement the between-source-and-gap shift, which minimizes
  flips to only the source-crossing case.

‚óŹ Now there's a consequence: with one-directional shift, the shift amount should
  be a full block height (not half), since blocks slide a full slot to fill the
  source's hole. Currently pm-shift-up/down is ¬ĪDRAG_SHIFT_PX/2 (¬Ī32). For the
  slot-fill model the shift should be roughly the source block's height ‚ÄĒ but
  using the fixed DRAG_SHIFT_PX keeps it universal. The gap that opens at the
  drop point will be DRAG_SHIFT_PX/2 (32px) since the between-blocks slide by
  32px. That's fine visually ‚ÄĒ the bar overlays it.

  Also, the source block (grey) no longer shifts in this model ‚ÄĒ it stays put
  while others slide around it. That's actually more correct (the source is
  "lifted" onto the cursor; its placeholder stays). Let me deploy and test.

  Ran 1 shell command (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Live on dev (still at 800ms so the motion is obvious).

  The real bug, finally pinned by your data: Block 11 on an up-move went U:-4.2 
  ‚Üí D:32 instantly ‚ÄĒ a block flipping from shift-up (‚ąí32) to shift-down (+32)
  while mid-transition. CSS transitions don't cleanly redirect when the target
  reverses sign in flight ‚ÄĒ the browser snaps. That snap was the pop. Down-moves
  happened to catch blocks at their settled endpoint (so the flip glided);
  up-moves caught them mid-glide (so it snapped). Floating the bar didn't touch
  this ‚ÄĒ it's a separate displacement-logic bug.

  The fix: displacement is now one-directional (SortableJS-style). Only the
  blocks between the dragged source and the drop gap shift, and they all slide
  the same direction to vacate the source's slot and open the drop slot. No
  block ever flips +32‚ÜĒ‚ąí32, so there's no mid-flight sign reversal to snap.

  CTL+F5 and drag up and down across several blocks. Both directions should
  glide smoothly now ‚ÄĒ no pop. (At 800ms it'll look slow/deliberate; once you
  confirm both directions glide, I'll set it back to a snappy production speed.)

  How does it read now?

‚óŹ This data is gold, and your verbal description nails it: "first jump glides, 
  then all paragraphs after that pop."

  Look at src=7 (dragging block 7 up), reading the gap going 6‚Üí5‚Üí4‚Üí3‚Üí2:
  gap=6: 6D:0   7*:0
  gap=5: 5D:32  6D:18.1   ‚Üź block 6 sweeping (gliding), block 5 INSTANTLY 32
  gap=4: 4D:32  5D:32  6D:27.2   ‚Üź block 4 instant 32, block 5 instant 32, block
  6 gliding
  gap=3: 3D:32  4D:32  5D:32  6D:28.9   ‚Üź only block 6 (nearest gap) glides;
  3,4,5 all instant 32

  The pattern is crystal clear: When the gap moves up, the block newly entering
  the shift range (the one nearest the gap) glides from 0‚Üí32. But every block
  that was already shifted is sitting at the settled 32. The problem: a block
  that just entered enters at 0 and on the NEXT gap-step it's already supposed
  to be at 32 ‚ÄĒ but it only had one frame, so it jumps to 32 with no transition
  because it goes from "not in range" (no class, transform 0) to "in range"
  (pm-shift-down, target 32) ‚ÄĒ and a brand-new class application with the
  element previously at 0... should transition 0‚Üí32.

  But it doesn't, because: when block N enters the range it's at 0, gets
  pm-shift-down, starts gliding 0‚Üí32. Then on the very next gap-step, block N+1
  enters and block N is now "deeper" in the range ‚ÄĒ still pm-shift-down, target
  still 32, so it just continues to 32. That should be fine.

  The actual snap: a block goes from 0 (no class) directly to its final 32 in 
  one sample ‚ÄĒ gap=4: 4D:32 appears at 32 immediately. Block 4 was not in range
  at gap=5 (it wasn't listed), then at gap=4 it's D:32 instantly. It entered the
  range and reached 32 in a single frame = no glide.

  Why down-moves glide but up-moves don't: On a down-move, blocks enter the
  range from the gap side ‚ÄĒ the block nearest the gap enters first and is the
  one that glides; as the gap moves down, that same nearest-block keeps gliding.
  On up-moves, the gap moves toward the source, and each newly-crossed block
  enters already needing to be at full shift because the blocks "behind" the gap
  are all already shifted ‚ÄĒ so a freshly-entering block has to instantly match
  its settled neighbors.

  The clean fix: the displacement shouldn't be "all-or-nothing per block." The
  real issue is the class is binary (0 or 32). When the gap sweeps, blocks
  should enter the shift gradually near the gap. But that's complex.

  The simpler, robust fix that matches what works: make the shift relative to 
  the gap distance isn't needed ‚ÄĒ instead, the proven-gliding direction (down)
  works because the gliding block is always the one adjacent to the gap. The fix
  is to ensure every newly-shifted block transitions from 0, which requires it
  NOT to appear already at 32.

  The root: a block entering the range gets pm-shift-down but if PM applies the
  class on a frame where the element is already laid out at the shifted position
  (because of how the gap jumped multiple indices), there's no 0-state frame.

  Let me reconsider with the simplest effective fix. The down-direction works
  perfectly. The asymmetry is purely in the order blocks enter. Let me check the
  relay and then apply: instead of fighting the per-block entry timing, mirror 
  the down-logic exactly for up ‚ÄĒ the down case shifts b.index >= gi (everything
  below gap) down, and the nearest-to-gap block glides. For up, I should shift
  everything in a way where the gap-adjacent block is the gliding one.

  Let me look at what's structurally different and fix it properly.

‚óŹ Now let me think about the actual mechanism, because the data shows the truth
  and I want to fix it right.

  In the src=7 up-drag, gap=5: 5D:32 (instant) 6D:18.1 (gliding). Block 6 is
  adjacent to the gap and glides. Block 5 is NOT adjacent ‚ÄĒ it's between gap(5)
  and source(7), so indices 5 and 6 are both in range [gi, si) = [5,7) = {5,6}.
  Block 5 just entered (gap went 6‚Üí5), block 6 was already in range from gap=6.

  Wait ‚ÄĒ at gap=6, src=7: range is [6,7) = {6}, so only block 6 shifts. At
  gap=5: range [5,7) = {5,6}. So block 5 NEWLY enters. And it shows 5D:32 ‚ÄĒ
  instant, no glide. Block 6 continues gliding (18.1).

  So the newly-entering block (5) pops to 32 instantly, while the continuing 
  block (6) glides. That's the bug: a block entering the shift range snaps to
  full shift instead of gliding from 0.

  Why? When block 5 enters, it gets pm-shift-down and should go 0‚Üí32. But it
  appears at 32 in the same frame it gets the class. The likely reason: the gap 
  jumped and block 5 got the class applied to a freshly-rendered/re-decorated 
  element that PM positioned, OR the transition needs the element to have
  rendered at 0 first.

  The robust fix per the research: a block entering should glide. The issue is
  the transition has no "from" because the class appears with the element
  already at target.

  The cleanest fix: don't make blocks bing-bang in/out of the range. Instead,
  ALL blocks between source and the furthest extent get shifted, and as the gap
  moves, blocks stay shifted (they don't pop in). But that changes the visual.

  Actually ‚ÄĒ the simplest correct fix, given down works: the down case shifts 
  b.index >= gi ‚ÄĒ i.e. a half-open range from the gap to the end of doc. That
  means when you move down, blocks LEAVE the range from the top (nearest source)
  and the gap-adjacent block is always freshly entering AND gliding. For up to
  work the same way, I need the up shift to also have the gliding block be the
  freshly-entering one.

  I think the truly robust fix is to abandon the binary class and make each 
  block's shift continuous based on its position relative to the gap ‚ÄĒ but that
  needs per-block values (which PM reverts if imperative, and rebuilds if
  decoration-style).

  Let me step back and use the pattern we KNOW works flawlessly: the bar's FLIP.
  The blocks should FLIP too. Let me reconsider ‚ÄĒ but the research said
  imperative writes get reverted. EXCEPT: the research also said
  FLIP-after-reorder works if done right.

  Given the complexity and that we've spent a lot here, let me propose the

‚óŹ I'll decide ‚ÄĒ and I'm going with the FLIP-in seed, because the data points to
  a precise, surgical fix and "ship with a known pop" isn't satisfying when
  we're this close.

  But let me reconsider the mechanism once more to make the fix bulletproof. The
  real question: why does a freshly-entering block appear at 32 with no
  0-frame?

  When block 5 enters the range, buildDecorations adds pm-shift-down to its
  decoration. PM patches the class onto the existing element in place. The
  element was at transform 0 (no class). Adding the class sets target +32. The
  browser should transition 0‚Üí32... unless the same update() cycle that adds the
  class also reads layout, forcing the browser to compute the +32 immediately
  and skip the transition's start.

  Actually ‚ÄĒ the most likely culprit: my own diagnostic and the bar's FLIP call 
  getComputedStyle/getBoundingClientRect/offsetHeight in the update hook, which
  forces synchronous style recalculation every frame. A forced reflow right
  after a class change can cause the browser to "snap" the transition's computed
  value to the target if the recalc happens before the transition registers the
  start value. The bar's void gapEl.offsetHeight and my diagnostic's
  getComputedStyle on every block could be eating the transition's 0-frame.

  That's testable and would explain the asymmetry (timing). Let me try the
  cheapest fix first: seed entering blocks at 0 explicitly. I'll do it in the
  update hook ‚ÄĒ detect blocks that just gained a shift class and weren't shifted
  last frame, force them to transform 0 with no transition, reflow, then let
  the class's transition take over.

  Let me implement the FLIP-in seed in the update hook.

‚óŹ My theory about the forced reflow: actually the simpler explanation given the
  data. Block 5 enters at gap=5 showing 32. But notice ‚ÄĒ it never showed a 0
  frame in the log because the diagnostic samples once per update(), and the
  block entered AND the next gap-change came before another sample. But the
  transition is 800ms ‚ÄĒ it can't complete in one frame. So if block 5 were
  transitioning 0‚Üí32, the NEXT sample (next gap move) would show it mid-glide
  (~5-10), not 32. It shows 32. So it genuinely jumped.

  The reason it jumps: when block 5 enters the range, the element gets
  pm-shift-down. But the element that gets the class may be at a position where 
  adding the class doesn't trigger a transition ‚ÄĒ because PM re-applied the
  WHOLE class list, and if the element was just re-rendered (PM patch), the
  browser sees it as initial state = target, no transition.

  The robust seed fix: track which blocks had a shift class last frame; for any
  block that is newly-shifted this frame, force transform: translateY(0) +
  transition: none, reflow, then remove the inline override so the class's
  transition fires from 0. Let me add this.

‚óŹ Live on dev (still 800ms for visibility).

  The bug, precisely from your data + description: "first jump glides, then all
  paragraphs after that pop." When you move up, each block that newly enters the
  shift range snaps straight to its full shift ‚ÄĒ the CSS transition has no
  0-frame to start from because PM applies the shift class with the element
  already at the shifted layout. The continuing/adjacent block always glides;
  only freshly-entering ones pop. Down-moves don't hit this because the gliding
  block is always the one already in range.

  The fix (FLIP-in seed): in the update hook, I detect blocks that newly gained
  a shift class versus last frame, and for each I force transform: 0 with no
  transition + a reflow, then hand back to the class ‚ÄĒ giving the transition a
  real 0‚Üítarget to animate. Already-shifted blocks are left untouched (their
  glide is in flight). Inline styles are cleared immediately so PM's observer
  has nothing to revert.

  CTL+F5 and drag up across several blocks. The freshly-entering paragraphs
  should now glide instead of popping ‚ÄĒ symmetric with the down direction.

  That seed approach was wrong ‚ÄĒ the research explicitly warned that imperative

  Here's where we honestly are after a long evening on this:

  imperative block writes (freezes) or per-block decoration styles

  Now the honest, traceable answer to "what happened to the up-glide?":

  Here's the sequence of what the displacement logic was at each point tonight:

  1. At "Good that is working" (float-overlay fix): the displacement was the
  original symmetric scheme ‚ÄĒ b.index >= gapIndex ‚Üí shift-down (+32), else ‚Üí 
  shift-up (‚ąí32). EVERY block shifted, splitting around the gap.
  2. Then you reported up-moves popping. I diagnosed it as the sign-flip (blocks
  flipping ‚ąí32‚ÜĒ+32 as the gap crosses them) and changed the logic to
  one-directional (only blocks between source and gap shift, one way).
  3. The one-directional change is what introduced the "freshly-entering block
  pops" behavior ‚ÄĒ and my FLIP-in seed fix for that froze Chrome.

‚óŹ Safe restore point committed: b9f0279. Everything from tonight is now
  recoverable ‚ÄĒ if my next attempt breaks things, git checkout b9f0279 -- <file>
  brings it all back.

  Now, the clean attempt. The root problem, stated precisely: a block that newly
  gets pm-shift-down/pm-shift-up has no transition start-frame, so it snaps. We
  can't fix it with imperative block writes (freezes) or per-block decoration
  styles (rebuilds).

  The research-backed approach that sidesteps all of it: don't move the blocks
  at all during the live drag. Instead, the gap is opened purely by the floating
  bar pushing a visual space, and the blocks stay put ‚ÄĒ OR, the cleaner
  version: render the displacement on a single transform on a wrapper/decoration
  that PM treats as one stable element.

  But actually, the simplest robust fix given everything we've learned: the 
  entering-block snap happens because the block goes from no-class (transform 0,
  no will-change) to shift (transform 32). If instead every block has the shift
  class applied from drag-start (all at transform 0 via a CSS variable
  defaulting to 0), and we only change a per-block variable ‚ÄĒ no, that's
  per-block style again.

  Let me think about the truly clean version and check the actual entering-block
  mechanism in code before I write anything.

  Read 1 file (ctrl+o to expand)

‚óŹ Here's my clean insight. The entering-block snap happens because a block
  transitions between three states: no-shift (0), shift-up (‚ąí32), shift-down
  (+32). A block "enters" by going from no-class to a shift class.

  The fix that needs NO imperative writes and NO per-block style: give EVERY
  block one of the two shift classes at all times during the drag ‚ÄĒ never "no
  class." A block is always either pm-shift-up or pm-shift-down based on which
  side of the gap it's on. That's already true for blocks in the symmetric
  scheme EXCEPT... actually in the symmetric scheme every block IS shifted
  (either up or down), there's no "no-class" middle state. So a block crossing
  the gap goes shift-up(‚ąí32) ‚Üí shift-down(+32) ‚ÄĒ a sign flip, which we
  established snaps.

  Wait ‚ÄĒ so it's the sign-flip after all, not the entering. The symmetric scheme
  has no "entering" (all blocks always shifted), only sign-flips. And the
  sign-flip snaps because CSS won't redirect a transition through a sign
  reversal cleanly.

  So the two schemes have two different failures:
  - Symmetric: every block always shifted ‚Üí sign-flips snap (the bug you see
  now).
  - One-directional: blocks enter/leave the range ‚Üí entering snaps.

  Both snap, different reasons. The unifying fix: the block must never change 
  its transform target abruptly.

  The genuinely clean solution: a 3-state class with intermediate won't help.
  What helps: make the transform a CSS variable that animates. But per-block
  variables = per-block style = rebuild.

  I think the honest answer is the research's conclusion: proper animated 
  displacement needs NodeViews or an overlay layer ‚ÄĒ the per-block-CSS approach
  has this inherent ceiling. That's a real, non-trivial build.

  Given the hour and that I've now got a committed safe point, let me be
  straight with you rather than start a NodeView rewrite at this depth tonight:

‚óŹ¬†User declined to answer questions

     symmetric scheme = blocks sign-flip across the gap (snaps); one-directional
     = blocks enter the range (snaps). The per-block-CSS approach has an 
     inherent ceiling here ‚ÄĒ the research's conclusion is that true animated 
     displacement needs NodeViews or an overlay layer, which is a real rebuild 
     (~not a quick patch). How do you want to spend the next effort? (NodeView 
     rebuild now / Simpler: thin insertion line / Stop, ship b9f0279)
